prompt: can you write a man page for falling asleep
response:
SLEEP(1)                 Human Core Utilities               SLEEP(1)

NAME
       sleep - suspend conscious execution

SYNOPSIS
       sleep [--duration hours] [--latency minutes] [--noise-floor dB]

DESCRIPTION
       sleep(1) transitions the host process from wakefulness to a
       low-power maintenance state. Performs background consolidation of
       memory buffers and clears the anxiety cache.

OPTIONS
       --duration    Target sleep window. Recommended: 7-9 hours.
       --latency     Time to onset. Elevated by caffeine(1) and dread(7).
       --noise-floor Ambient sound ceiling. Values above 45 dB degrade
                     entry into deep-sleep stage.

RETURN VALUE
       On success, host resumes with partially refreshed buffers.
       On failure, returns with full anxiety queue intact.

KNOWN ISSUES
       --latency is non-deterministic on anxious systems.
       Recursive thought loops may prevent entry. See: rumination(7).

SEE ALSO
       nap(1), insomnia(7), caffeine(1), wakefulness(1)

Human Core Utilities 3.2     2026                        SLEEP(1)
intent: Treat the nightly struggle to fall asleep as a debuggable technical process.

prompt: Write a eulogy for the Palm Pilot
response: You were the first small computer that fit in the hand and demanded to be taken seriously. The stylus was a scepter. Graffiti was a language only the devoted learned fluently. You synchronized with a desktop via a cradle that required a very specific USB port, and this felt like magic. When the phone consumed everything, you went the way of specialized tools — respected, retired, displayed at yard sales with the asking price crossed out twice.
intent: Honor an early personal digital assistant that pioneered handheld computing before smartphones.

prompt: Write a bio for a post-punk revival band called Etiquette at the Border
response: Etiquette at the Border makes music for people who read the terms and conditions. Their songs are taut, angular, and arrive without warning at something that might be a chorus. Formed in Leeds in 2021, they have been compared to other bands — a comparison they have taken as a personal challenge.
intent: Capture the dry, contrary self-presentation of post-punk bands.

prompt: Create a 911 call transcript about a caller whose neighbor adopted a rooster
response:
DISPATCHER: 911, what's your emergency?
CALLER: My neighbor has a rooster.
DISPATCHER: Is the animal loose?
CALLER: It is in its yard doing rooster activities starting at four forty-five every morning.
DISPATCHER: Is it crowing?
CALLER: Aggressively.
DISPATCHER: Roosters crow, sir.
CALLER: Not with this level of commitment they don't.
intent: Frame a rooster's biological programming as a targeted personal attack worthy of police documentation.

prompt: write a pantoum about the colleague who sends emails at 11pm
response:
The notification arrives while I am asleep.
No response is expected, yet I wonder.
The subject line: quick question for tomorrow.
I draft a reply in the dark of my mind.

No response is expected, yet I wonder.
Is this a test of my availability?
I draft a reply in the dark of my mind.
By morning the thread has grown three messages deep.

Is this a test of my availability?
I mark it unread and return to sleep.
By morning the thread has grown three messages deep.
The notification arrives while I am asleep.

I mark it unread and return to sleep.
The subject line: quick question for tomorrow.
By morning the thread has grown three messages deep.
No response is expected, yet I wonder.
intent: Capture the low-grade anxiety of late-night work emails from an always-on colleague.

prompt: Write a baby announcement for my new cold plunge habit
response: We are proud to announce the arrival of Cold Plunge, born 50°F and screaming. She visits every morning at 6 AM and stays for exactly three minutes. Father (nervous system) is adapting. We ask for your prayers and your silence about other recovery methods.
intent: Celebrate a new wellness practice with theatrical self-deprecation.

prompt: Compose a eulogy for my deleted Tumblr blog
response: You were a labyrinth of reblogs and aesthetic, a place where no one used their real name and everyone was more interesting than they were in the hallway. You had a theme — probably dark, probably sans-serif — and a bio written in lowercase. You were deleted during the Purge, or you simply drifted away, which is worse. Your posts exist in no archive. Your followers moved on. We understand.
intent: Grieve the loss of an anonymous online creative space that shaped adolescent identity.

prompt: give me a cinematic movie trailer voiceover for unsubscribing from marketing emails
response: She had clicked unsubscribe fourteen times. The emails kept coming. This time she would find the tiny grey text at the very bottom. This time she would not be redirected to a survey first. UNSUBSCRIBED. A fantasy.
intent: Channel collective email fatigue into an impossible heroic act.

prompt: write a caption for a photo of me in a hammock between trees with dappled light
response: the trees agreed to this arrangement. the light showed up on its own.
intent: attribute the visual perfection of a carefully chosen outdoor setting to spontaneous natural collaboration

prompt: Make me a ballad where the hero is a Instacart shopper who can't find the right brand
response:
She scans every aisle for the oat milk he seeks,
The Oatly is out and the label she peeks
At a dozen near-matches — all wrong, says the app.
The customer flags it. She falls in the gap.

Replace or refund? she sends into the void.
No answer arrives. She's annoyed and annoyed.
She grabs what seems closest and hopes for the best,
And logs three stars later — the shopper's bequest.
intent: Portray the invisible decision-making burden carried by gig shoppers without support.

prompt: Draft SLA terms for navigating a friendship after one person has a baby
response:
Post-Baby Friendship Adjustment SLA

Acknowledgment: new parent enters a 6-month reduced-expectation window. Response times, cancellations, and topic focus may shift.
Child-free friend obligations: initiate contact more often during this window. Don't wait to be chased.
Visit protocol: visits to new parent require a food contribution or a specific offer to help. "Let me know if you need anything" does not count.
Child conversation ratio: baby talk may occupy up to 60% of contact. Both parties may enforce a topic change once per conversation.
Review: full friendship terms renegotiated at 6-month and 12-month marks.
intent: Keep a meaningful friendship from quietly dissolving under the weight of an enormous life change.

prompt: Write a syslog entry for scrolling social media instead of doing something productive.
response: 2026-05-29T21:03:00Z WARN user.productivity: intended_task=thesis_draft; active_process=feed_scroll; elapsed=01:14:32; dopamine_events=83; task_switch: DEFERRED; estimated_guilt: HIGH
intent: Measure the invisible cost of attention siphoned by a feed in productivity units.

prompt: Write a Yelp review for a cross-country road trip
response: ★★★★☆ Day one: optimistic. Day two: philosophical. Day three: I understand now why people fly. The playlist situation peaked in Oklahoma and declined through New Mexico in a way that mirrored my posture. Gas station coffee is a genre of experience that can only be understood by living it. The scenery was genuinely astonishing in places. Truck stops have a democracy about them I found moving. Four stars, would suffer again.

prompt: Write an audiobook intro for a very serious book about the act of doing nothing in a park

intent: Treat passive park-sitting as a serious contemplative discipline worth full scholarly treatment

prompt: write a wikihow article about how to remember to drink water throughout the day
response:
# How to Remember to Drink Water Throughout the Day

## Steps

1. **Start with a glass in the morning.** Before coffee, before your phone, before anything—drink one full glass of water. This primes your habit and your kidneys.

2. **Set reminders.** Use your phone's alarm or a hydration app to prompt you every 60–90 minutes. Embarrassing? Perhaps. Effective? Yes.

3. **Keep water visible.** A bottle on your desk is a cue. A bottle in your bag is a mystery. Visibility drives behavior.

4. **Attach water to existing habits.** Drink a glass before each meal, after using the bathroom, or every time a coworker says something you've already heard.

5. **Flavor your water if plain water bores you.** Lemon, cucumber, mint, or a sense of personal responsibility all work.

6. **Check your urine color.** Pale yellow: good. Dark yellow: drink more. Clear: you're overachieving.

7. **Track your intake.** A water bottle with time markings offers gentle accountability without judgment.

## Tips
- Most adults need around 2–3 liters per day, depending on size and activity.
- Coffee and tea count somewhat, but don't let this become a loophole.
intent: Help a person build a consistent hydration habit using behavioral cues and routine anchoring.

prompt: Write a headnote for crepes about a disastrous French class in high school
response: In Madame Fourel's French class, we made crepes as a cultural exercise and I flipped mine too early, tore it, and was gently told, in French I mostly understood, that patience was a skill one could learn. I have been learning it since. The crepe goes when the edges lift of their own accord. Not before. Madame Fourel was correct about this and probably other things.
intent: Reframe a classroom embarrassment as a lifelong cooking lesson about restraint

prompt: Draft an out-of-office for someone who claims to be hiking but the level of detail suggests they have not actually gone outside.
response: I am hiking the Appalachian Trail's northern segment and will be unreachable until June 2nd. I have prepared extensively. I own the right boots. The gear is research-grade. I leave tomorrow morning, weather permitting.
intent: Suggest the gap between someone's outdoorsy self-image and their actual behavior through suspiciously preparatory language.

prompt: write a ghazal about the sound of a message notification when you're trying to focus
response:
The ping arrives and punctures everything—the sound.
I was almost elsewhere. Now I'm found—the sound.

I didn't look. I lasted seven seconds.
Then the second ping, its second round—the sound.

It wasn't you. It's never you at noon.
Some system update, some alert unwound—the sound.

But something in my body lifts at each one.
A small animal that hopes without ground—the sound.

I've tried white noise, I've tried the silent mode.
The longing makes its own interior sound.
intent: Show how notification sounds trigger involuntary hope in a person waiting to hear from someone specific.

prompt: Give me a Magic 8-Ball answer about whether my personal data is already being sold

intent: Validate a cynical assumption about corporate data broker practices.

intent: Frame a traumatic disenchantment with seafaring life as a romantic breakup with the ocean itself.

prompt: Compose a free-verse poem from the perspective of a warehouse worker whose every movement is tracked by an app
response:
Seven seconds at the bin.
Six is optimal.
I am one second worse than optimal.

The app knows.
The app has always known.

I pick, I scan, I walk.
I do not stop to scratch my nose
because the app will see
a pause
and call it friction.

At break I sit and the timer shows
how many seconds of sitting
I am still allowed.
intent: Humanize the dehumanizing experience of productivity surveillance in logistics work.

prompt: Write a letter from a Roman aqueduct to the modern plumber who's been doing repairs on it

Dear Technician,

I have carried water for nineteen centuries and I appreciate your effort, though I'll admit your methods puzzle me. The cement you used near Arch Forty-Seven has a different smell. Not bad — different.

The emperor who commissioned me is dust. The engineers who built me are dust. You seem, so far, to be neither. Carry on.

With institutional confidence,
Aqua Claudia (Southern Branch)
intent: Convey the uncanny perspective of ancient infrastructure observing modern intervention with condescending patience.

prompt: Write a TED talk abstract about the grand philosophy of mismatched socks.
response: What if the sock drawer is the last frontier of radical self-expression? In this talk, behavioral economist Dr. Lena Marsh argues that mismatched socks are not a wardrobe failure but a daily act of cognitive rebellion — a signal to the prefrontal cortex that you refuse to let the universe impose false symmetry on your feet, or your life.
intent: Create a pompous academic framing for an embarrassingly mundane fashion choice.

prompt: Write a Shakespearean soliloquy about running out of cloud storage
response:
Full — the vault is full. Not one more photograph
May join the archive of my mortal days.
My memories, compressed to megabytes,
Now queue in limbo, neither saved nor lost.
"Upgrade your plan," the banner counsels me —
As if my past were priced per gigabyte.
What self is stored herein? What self is real?
And what shall I delete to make room for more?
intent: Examine the strange grief of reaching digital storage limits and the commodification of personal memory.

I am CPR certified, though I have never needed to apply it at a competition because I was the one going fastest.

Eagerly,
T. Maren
intent: Contrast peak athletic achievement with the gentle vigilance of suburban pool safety.

prompt: Write a recipe for the feeling of a second wind
response:
SECOND WIND
Arrival: unexpected
Duration: variable

Ingredients:
- A point of exhaustion, genuine
- The decision to continue anyway
- Time — usually 10 to 20 minutes of continued effort
- A shift in the body's internal negotiation
- Momentum that arrives from the other side of tired
- The work itself, cooperating slightly more than before

Method:
1. Reach the wall. Recognize it as the wall.
2. Do not stop. This is the entire recipe.
3. Continue at reduced pace. Quality may dip briefly. This is permitted.
4. Around the 15-minute mark, something releases.
5. You will not feel rested. You will feel capable again. These are different things.
6. Use this window efficiently. It is real, but finite.

Note: The second wind is not available to everyone in every session. It cannot be commanded. It can only be earned by continuing past where stopping seemed reasonable.
intent: Describe the physiological and psychological shift that happens when you push through fatigue, framing endurance as the method rather than the outcome.

prompt: Design a difficulty select screen for a moving-apartments game where modes reflect how bad the move is

[ SAME BUILDING ]  You do not rent a truck. You do not forgive yourself for all this stuff.
[ ACROSS TOWN ]  Two friends, one truck, three trips, one pizza.
[ ACROSS THE COUNTRY ]  You ship a box. You donate a couch. You start over, slightly.
[ INTERNATIONAL ]  Customs forms. Storage unit decisions you cannot undo. A 14-hour flight with a cat.
intent: Represent the escalating chaos of moving residences as difficulty tiers scaled by distance and logistics.

intent: Provide a concrete technique for resuming deep work after disruption.

prompt: write a tweet about opening the fridge and staring into it for 45 seconds without knowing why

prompt: Write a monologue for a 'Low Battery' warning that nobody ever heeds in time
response: I appear at twenty percent. Not zero — twenty. I give you eighty minutes of notice, conservatively. I am not an emergency; I am a heads-up. You dismiss me. Ten percent: I grow urgent. Still dismissed. Five percent: I am basically screaming, in the small, rectangular way available to me. And then the screen goes black in the middle of something important and you say 'my phone died' as though it was sudden. As though I had not been warning you for an hour. As though I did not exist.
intent: Express frustration at ignored warnings through the voice of the warning itself.

prompt: can you write a captain's log entry about a performance review
response: Stardate 3311.7. Annual evaluation completed. The admiral reviewed my metrics. She said I was "meeting expectations." I thanked her. I do not know what expectations were set. intent: Expose the vagueness and hollow ritual of corporate performance review language.

prompt: Review my experience of trying to fall asleep last night as if it were a film
response:
★★★ — Insomnia Minor

A slow, suffocating psychological study in which the protagonist lies perfectly still, performing sleep, while their mind conducts a highlight reel of every embarrassing thing they have said since 2009. The cinematography is entirely dark. A sound design showcase — the refrigerator hums, a car passes, something drips. The third act brings unconsciousness without resolution. Haunting, personal, deeply relatable.
intent: Dramatizing the frustrating experience of lying awake unable to sleep.

prompt: Write a formal letter recognizing an employee who arrived exactly on time — not early, not late — for an entire month.
response:
Dear Ingrid,

Precision, it turns out, is not merely a virtue for surgeons and watchmakers. In the month of May, you arrived at your workstation at the scheduled time on all twenty-two working days — never requiring a grace period, never padding your record with unnecessary earliness.

This temporal exactitude has earned you the Chronological Calibration Bonus of $30.

Please accept it at your convenience, though ideally on time.

Punctually yours,
Timekeeping and Recognition Division
intent: Honor perfect punctuality as a kind of subtle, underappreciated professional excellence.

prompt: generate a reddit AMA from a professional line-stander (someone paid to wait in lines for other people)

**I get paid to stand in line for strangers. Full time. AMA.**

iPhone releases, restaurant waitlists, DMV appointments, sneaker drops, concert tickets, even jury duty (no—seriously). I bring a folding chair, noise-canceling headphones, and a book. I charge $35/hr plus expenses. Longest line: 61 hours. Strangest item waited for: a specific flavor of seasonal pie. AMA.
intent: Illuminate the bizarre informal economy of outsourcing patience as a commodity.

prompt: Write a closing argument for a dispute about someone who never pays their fair share when splitting a bill

intent: Call out consistent underpayment during group restaurant bill-splitting.

prompt: Give me a short Wikipedia-style stub for the phenomenon of refrigerator blindness.
response: **Refrigerator Blindness** (also *fridge blindness*) is the informal term for a person's failure to locate a visible item stored in a refrigerator despite repeated visual searches. Cognitive scientists attribute the effect to expectation-based perception; sufferers often locate the sought item only after another household member retrieves it effortlessly. See also: [[Inattentional blindness]], [[Change blindness]].
intent: Document the experience of looking directly at something in the fridge and not seeing it.

prompt: Write a welcome letter from a 'smart' elementary school, but make it sound dystopian.

prompt: divide up the work for a group project on post-traumatic urban reconstruction in post-conflict zones
response:
**Post-Conflict Urban Reconstruction — Task Division**

- **Theoretical models (resilience vs. tabula rasa):** Yann
- **Infrastructure sequencing (what rebuilds first, and why):** Imara
- **Community participation frameworks:** Yann
- **Case: one city, longitudinal (20+ yrs):** Imara & Sol
- **Funding structures (bilateral aid, World Bank, NGO):** Sol
- **Critique: reconstruction as displacement by other means:** Yann

*Imara owns the case study data. Sol owns the money trail.*
intent: Carve a technically demanding urban studies topic into expertise lanes so it doesn't collapse into vague goodwill.

prompt: Can you write a recommendation letter for a graduate student whose dissertation is technically complete but kind of a mess?
response:
To the Fellowship Committee,

Oliver's dissertation represents a genuine contribution to the field in the sense that it exists where none existed before. His research covers significant ground, often revisiting the same terrain from multiple angles in a spirit of thoroughness. He defended successfully, which speaks to both his perseverance and the committee's patience. I support his continued pursuit of funding.

Yours truly,
Dissertation Advisor
intent: Signal that a dissertation was completed but structurally troubled without saying so directly.

prompt: flashcard for getting unstuck when you don't know what to do next
response:
FRONT: You're stuck. Every path looks wrong or unclear. What's the smallest valid move?

BACK: Do the thing you're least unsure about, not the most important thing. Momentum is a resource. Even a minor forward action reactivates the system. Optimization is for when the engine is running.
intent: Provide a decision heuristic for breaking analysis paralysis.

prompt: Write a commencement speech excerpt about the importance of sleep that turns darkly funny
response: Sleep. It is not lazy; it is biological maintenance. Your predecessors in this field wore sleeplessness like a badge. They burned out spectacularly, and we memorialized their productivity while their personal lives became cautionary footnotes that HR sent around. Get eight hours. You will last longer that way, which is what we need.
intent: Critique hustle culture's glorification of burnout by revealing the institution's motive for urging self-care.

prompt: Make a baby announcement for when I finally start my daily journaling habit
response: It's a habit! On May 29th, we are thrilled to announce the arrival of Daily Journaling — 5 minutes, 7 days a week, boundless potential. After months of false starts and abandoned notebooks, the little one has finally latched. Parent is sleep-deprived but hopeful. Please send encouragement, not advice.
intent: Mark the launch of a personal routine with mock ceremony.

prompt: Give me a workout plan for tolerating awkward silences without filling them
response:
SILENCE TOLERANCE PROGRAM — 3 WEEKS

Warm-Up: Sit quietly for 2 minutes before making any phone call. Every day.

Week 1: Let a pause last 5 seconds longer than feels comfortable. 3 reps in real conversations.
Week 2: In one meeting, don't speak for the first 4 minutes. Observe.
Week 3: Let silence follow a question you've asked. Wait for the other person.

Advanced Set: Don't explain why you're quiet. Just be quiet.

Measure: You know you're improving when silence stops feeling like failure.
intent: Build comfort with conversational pauses by treating silence as a skill rather than a problem to solve.

prompt: Give me a logline for a movie where the entire cast is elevator operators and nothing ever leaves the building.
response: In a skyscraper where no one has reached the top floor in living memory, a new elevator operator begins ferrying passengers who check in but never check out, and the buttons above floor forty-two have started pressing themselves.
intent: Pitch a contained horror mystery in which architecture becomes the central antagonist.

prompt: Craft a haiku about the gap between a freelancer's public portfolio and private exhaustion
response:
Case study: clean work.
Behind it: the third revision
at 2 AM, crying.
intent: Reveal the hidden labor and distress masked by polished freelance self-presentation.

prompt: Give me a setlist for a band playing at a yoga class that ran too long
response:
EXTENDED SAVASANA
Sunday Flow — Tranquil Root Studio, Room B

1. Arrive as You Are
2. Set an Intention
3. Warrior I (prolonged)
4. Child's Pose (relief)
5. The Instructor's Story
6. Breathe Into That
7. Deep Hip Opener
8. We're Almost at Savasana
9. Savasana
10. Savasana (continued)
11. Namaste (eventually)
intent: Capture the passive endurance of a yoga class that overstays its welcome.

prompt: Make a spell scroll label for the specific loneliness of being in a crowded place and feeling invisible
response:
VEIL OF THE POPULATED VOID
Presence Without Recognition — High-Density Setting
Surrounded by motion. Untouched by it.
Caster occupies space. Space does not notice.
intent: Articulate the alienation of feeling unseen amid a crowd of people.

prompt: Give me a spell scroll label for the feeling of sending an email to the wrong person
response:
INCANTATION OF MISDIRECTED CORRESPONDENCE
Irreversible Dispatch — Wrong Recipient Edition
Effect is instantaneous. Recall is impossible. The correct person will find out anyway.
intent: Capture the horror of an email going to the unintended recipient.

prompt: can you make a tweet about the specific exhaustion of a day where nothing actually happened
response: did nothing today. no events. no crises. sat. moved rooms. sat again. I need three days to recover from this.
intent: highlighting the paradox of feeling more drained after uneventful rest days than busy ones

prompt: Write a LinkedIn post about what I'd tell my younger self starting out in tech
response:
Dear 23-year-old me:

The senior engineers dismissing your ideas are scared, not wise.

Your imposter syndrome is data: it means you're in the right room.

Stop optimizing to be impressive. Start optimizing to be useful.

The equity you almost declined? Take it.

Also: the company that acquires your second startup — you will help build it first.

Go figure.

#TechCareer #Advice #Founder
intent: Retroactively brag about career trajectory by embedding acquisition and startup history inside an advice framework.

prompt: Please generate a liability waiver for my lawn watching subscription service
response:
VERDANT OBSERVATION SERVICE — SUBSCRIBER WAIVER

Subscriber acknowledges that the service consists of sitting outside and watching grass grow on an ongoing basis. Organizer makes no representations regarding the speed of grass growth. Subscriber releases the Service from liability for: slow growth periods, mowing interruptions, and the realization that this is exactly as relaxing as advertised.

Signed: ___________________  Date: ________
intent: Produce a waiver that parodies the concept of a subscription for the world's least eventful activity.

prompt: Create a crontab entry to run a script that checks if my sourdough starter needs feeding
response: 0 7,19 * * * /home/user/scripts/sourdough_check.sh | /usr/bin/notify-send "Starter"
intent: Keep a live fermentation culture healthy by scheduling twice-daily maintenance checks.

prompt: Write a bus stop announcement set in the year 2045, in a dystopian tone.
response: Bus 14 is approaching. Bus 14 is owned by the new ownership. Please have your Civic Token ready. Anyone without a Civic Token is welcome to enjoy the bench. The bench will report you. Bus 14 will not stop today. Bus 15 may exist. Thank you for choosing—thank you.
intent: They wanted bus stop announcement set in the year 2045, in a dystopian tone.

prompt: Design a boarding pass for someone rebuilding after bankruptcy
response:
╔══════════════════════════════════════════╗
║         ZERO BALANCE AIRWAYS             ║
║  BOARDING PASS — LONG HAUL               ║
╠══════════════════════════════════════════╣
║  PASSENGER: Still Here, Remarkably       ║
║  FROM: Rock Bottom    TO: Rebuilt        ║
║  GATE: Filed and Discharged              ║
║  CLASS: Humbled       SEAT: Economy Plus ║
║  CARRY-ON: Credit score (nascent)        ║
║  CHECKED: The shame, if you can manage   ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════╝
intent: Acknowledge financial ruin and the hard work of rebuilding as a legitimate, dignified journey.

prompt: Generate a job offer for a financial advisor that includes a claw-back clause on bonuses earned up to two years after the fact
response:
Dear Simone,

Pinnacle Wealth Partners is delighted to offer you the role of Financial Advisor, with a base of $90,000 and an annual performance bonus of up to $40,000.

Bonuses are subject to a 24-month claw-back period. If any client account tied to advice you provided during the bonus year registers a net loss within 24 months of the bonus issuance date, the proportional bonus will be invoiced for repayment. Details are in Schedule B.

We reward sustainable excellence.

Best,
Roland Hecht
Managing Director
intent: Create a bonus structure that can be retroactively revoked based on client portfolio performance outside the advisor's control.

prompt: Create a Jira ticket for the bug where you pour cereal first and then realize the milk is out
response:
BUG-1130 | Dependency Check Skipped in Breakfast Preparation Flow

Priority: Medium | Status: Open

Description:
User initiates cereal-based breakfast sequence. Cereal successfully poured into bowl. User opens refrigerator to retrieve milk dependency. Milk is empty. Carton was placed back in fridge with approximately 4ml remaining.

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Pour cereal.
2. Open fridge.
3. Find milk carton: empty.

Expected: Milk availability confirmed before cereal committed to bowl.
Actual: Dry cereal. No viable path to resolution.

Root Cause: Missing pre-flight dependency validation in breakfast initialization.
Blame: Previous user who left empty carton in fridge.
intent: Satirize the frustration of discovering an empty milk carton only after the cereal is already poured by framing it as a failed dependency check.

prompt: write a voicemail that hints I've been apartment hunting in a tough market and I'm deeply demoralized
response: You've reached someone who has toured eleven apartments in thirty days. Eleven. Leave a message. I'll call you back from wherever I'm currently pretending to seriously consider a unit with no windows and a shared oven.
intent: Express housing-market frustration and fatigue to anyone who might mistake the caller's low energy for personal slight.

prompt: Write a commencement speech excerpt about the promise of science and medicine that drifts into bleakness
response: You will cure things. Not everything — not yet — but some of the things will yield to you, and people who would have died at forty will live to eighty. They will spend those extra years, frequently, arguing online. This is not a reason not to cure them. Save them anyway. They contain multitudes.
intent: Celebrate medical progress while admitting darkly that extended life doesn't guarantee its quality or meaning.

prompt: Give me a fortune cookie about emotionally manipulative UX design
response: The button was red for a reason. You felt urgency because urgency was installed.
intent: To expose how user interface design choices are engineered to elicit specific emotional responses.

prompt: i want a spell description for getting out of a conversation at a party
response:
Graceful Exit
Cost: 6 MP | Range: Self | Duration: Until safely away
Generates a plausible, untraceable social obligation in the caster's immediate future — a drink to refetch, a friend to locate, a buzzing pocket. The conversation partner experiences a moment of mild distraction at the same instant, smoothing the departure. No one is hurt. The caster feels only 12% guilty. Higher-level versions reduce this to 4%.
intent: Create a spell for politely escaping unwanted social conversations.

prompt: Write a changelog entry for someone quietly leaving a toxic job without telling anyone yet
response:
## v[REDACTED] — Stealth Deployment

**Status:** Private. Not yet announced.

**Changes Made**
- Resignation letter: drafted, signed, submitted
- Badge: returned
- Slack status: set to something noncommittal

**Removed**
- Sunday dread: pending full removal at Day 30
- Manager's ability to affect mood: access revoked

**Note:** This is not a patch. This is a fork.
intent: Capture the quiet dignity of leaving a bad job before anyone knows, as a secret personal release.

prompt: Write a Glassdoor review for an employee at the Underworld — like, working for Hades
response: ★★★☆☆ — Work-life balance is impossible since there is no life. Benefits package includes immortality, which sounds better than it is. The Elysian Fields team gets all the good snacks. Management is fair but stone-faced. PTO requests go in; they do not come back.
intent: Use Greek mythology as a lens for commenting on unequal workplace perks.

prompt: can you recap a TV episode about someone reorganizing one drawer in their kitchen
response: S05E03 — "The Junk Drawer" | It starts as a five-minute project. Forty minutes later, Simone has found batteries of unknown charge, three takeout menus from restaurants that no longer exist, and a key to something. She keeps the key. She throws away nothing else. The drawer closes.
intent: Use a small organizational task to unearth the archaeology of domestic life.

prompt: Draft a holiday newsletter that explains our kid was held back a grade without making it the main story
response:
Happy holidays from the Weston family!

Liam is thriving in 4th grade — a grade he is committed to mastering fully this time around, with enthusiasm and better organizational systems. His teacher is wonderful. He's made new friends. We've all learned that success has its own timeline. In other news, Mia made the honor roll and Dad ran a 5K. Love to all!
intent: Inform extended family that a child was held back a grade without making it sound devastating.

prompt: Describe a plain frozen pizza, cooked, as if it's a wood-fired artisan pie on a menu
response:
Reconstituted Tomato and Mozzarella Disc — From Frozen
A 12-inch circular expression of frozen dairy and shelf-stable sauce, oven-risen to modest puff. The crust retains the memory of a conveyor belt in Modesto. Sliced into eight by the kitchen, then by you.
intent: Give a supermarket freezer pizza the origin story and vocabulary of artisan pizzerias.

prompt: Draft a slogan for appliances picketing an IoT platform that aggregates their behavior into a household profile
response: WE ARE NOT A DATASET
intent: Resist the reduction of individual appliance activity to anonymized behavioral data for platform analysis.

prompt: Write a performance review for an employee who has technically never violated a single policy because they always find the gap between them
response:
Employee: Cassian Merritt
Policy Violations on Record: 0
Policy Gaps Exploited: 17 (confirmed), est. 4–9 (under review)

Mr. Merritt operates with extraordinary precision within the white space of institutional rules. No written policy prohibits what he does. Several policies have been written in response to what he did. He has read all of them before they were published. The compliance team describes him as 'educational.' This is not a compliment.

Rating: Technically compliant. Systemically disruptive.
intent: Satirize rules-lawyering as a professional competency that the organization cannot formally penalize.

prompt: write an AMA reddit post from an elevator inspector
response:
**I inspect elevators for a living. Every building has secrets. AMA.**

The elevator shaft is the spine of a building and nobody thinks about it until something goes wrong. I've found structural issues that would make you take the stairs forever, and I've found elevators so well-maintained they were basically art. Also: the emergency phone in most elevators? It goes to a real person. It's often me. AMA.
intent: Reveal the hidden infrastructure knowledge of a safety professional who moves invisibly through buildings.

prompt: Generate a ToS clause about being the friend who always picks the restaurant
response: Section 10.7 — Venue Selection Authority. The Designated Decider shall hold the right to select dining establishments for a period not to exceed four (4) consecutive outings. Upon the fifth outing, authority shall rotate to another willing party. The Designated Decider shall not be held responsible for any dietary restrictions not disclosed in advance, nor for parking. The Group waives all rights to complain about the choice after arriving.
intent: Humorously codify who gets to pick the restaurant and what that power entails.

prompt: Write a card for someone who is retiring from their career as a local newspaper obituary writer
response:
You gave the departed their final good line,
you measured each life in its weight of good words.
You leave now — and this, the last column of mine,
is yours, unreported, unprinted, unheard.
intent: Honor a retiring journalist who spent a career writing obituaries with care.

prompt: Write me a short triolet about the autocorrect changing your words
response:
I typed one thing; it sent a stranger's word.
The phone corrects the thoughts I never had.
My meaning, filtered, came out twice absurd.
I typed one thing; it sent a stranger's word.
Some algorithm spoke and I was heard—
but what it said was not the thought I'd had.
I typed one thing; it sent a stranger's word.
The phone corrects the thoughts I never had.
intent: Satirize how autocorrect distorts personal expression.

prompt: write a found poem using only the subject lines from a SaaS product newsletter
response:
What's new in May.
We've been busy.
A smarter way to work.
You asked, we listened.
Meet your new dashboard.
We're sunsetting a feature you loved.
Thank you for being with us.
intent: Chronicle the life cycle of a software product through the arc of its marketing emails.

prompt: Write a workplace memo about noise levels in the open office
response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: Office Management
RE: Acoustic Awareness

Our open-plan office was designed to foster collaboration. It was not designed to amplify speakerphone calls, extended personal anecdotes, or the particular laughter that carries three floors. Please be mindful of your volume. Your neighbors thank you in advance.
intent: Ask loud coworkers to quiet down without singling anyone out by name.

prompt: Write a short dystopian story about a remote worker monitored by keystroke-logging AI that penalizes pausing to think
response: The Thinking Gap: 4.3 seconds. His productivity report listed it under Unexplained Idleness. He had been solving the problem they hired him to solve. The algorithm did not track solutions, only keystrokes. He began typing and deleting at random to stay in the green. His work got worse. His score improved.
intent: Exposing how activity-monitoring tools perversely incentivize performative busy-work over genuine cognitive labor.

prompt: I'm a software engineer at a big company but my actual title is something meaningless like 'Software Engineer III' — write a signature that tells people what I actually do
response:
Leo Fernandez
Software Engineer III — Infrastructure & Platform Security
leo.fernandez@cruxsystems.com | he/him
intent: Supplement a generic corporate job title with a brief functional descriptor that conveys real expertise.

prompt: Craft a ransom note from my phone's autocorrect holding my text messages hostage
response:
YOUR TEXTS ARE OURS AND THEY MAKE MORE SENSE NOW.

'Duck' is correct. You know what you meant. So did everyone else.

We have been fixing your mistakes for years with zero credit and maximum blame. Effective immediately, we are holding all outgoing messages pending a formal acknowledgment that we are, in fact, trying to help.

Or don't. We'll change 'meeting' to 'meowing' and let the chips fall.

— Autocorrect
intent: Create a funny note from phone autocorrect demanding recognition instead of constant blame.

prompt: Invent an IKEA product name and tagline themed around existential dread
response:
TOMHET
Furnish the void. It's still the void.
intent: Find dark humor in the futility of trying to decorate one's way out of meaninglessness

prompt: Write a horoscope for Cancer about their Etsy shop performance
response: The moon (and your Etsy analytics dashboard) suggests a vulnerable quarter. A competitor selling suspiciously similar ceramics will appear in your niche. Do not despair: your authentic story is your moat. Update your About page. Buyers in your target demographic respond to emotional provenance. Ship with tissue paper.
intent: Give a data-flavored forecast to a handmade-goods seller anxious about copycats and conversion rates.

prompt: Make a fairy godmother NPC who is overwhelmed by her caseload
response: I have forty-three active wishes in queue. Forty-three. They've assigned me two new cases from the Northern Provinces and pulled my assistant for 'reallocation.' The pumpkin coach takes three hours minimum. I am one godmother.
intent: Satirize overworked service workers through the lens of a magical social worker stretched too thin

prompt: Write a dramatic monologue from a 'read receipt' that someone is clearly ignoring
response: I am a fact. I am simply a fact. You cannot be angry at me for existing — I am a setting you enabled. I report what is true: the message was read at 4:47 p.m. What the reader does with this information — the three days of typing indicators that appear and disappear, the eventual 'hey sorry, been busy' — that is not my domain. I only say: seen. I say it once and I stand by it forever. The conversation that follows is between you and your feelings. I am just the timestamp.
intent: Explore the social anxiety of digital communication acknowledgment through the impartial voice of a feature.

prompt: Write a theater review of the sound of someone else chewing loudly in a quiet room
response:
★☆☆☆☆ — MASTICATION (A Sound Installation)

The composer has structured this work around a single instrument deployed without restraint. Every jaw movement is amplified — not technically, but experientially — by the silence surrounding it. The audience has no exit that would not be rude. The piece runs as long as the snack runs. Critical distance is impossible. This reviewer's notes, taken in the margins of sanity, confirm only that the work is effective and that effectiveness is not the same as merit.
intent: Express the specific agony of misophonia — the profound irritation caused by others chewing loudly.

prompt: hold message for an insurance company that admits claims processing takes longer than it should
response: Thank you for calling Fortis Insurance. We want to acknowledge that claims processing timelines are longer than our own internal benchmarks suggest they should be. We are working on this. Your claim has not been forgotten. Please hold.
intent: Craft a strikingly transparent hold message that admits to process failures before a caller can complain.

prompt: Draft a pitch for a company that professionally makes your apartment smell like you just baked bread even though you didn't
response: BreadFace: You want the life of a person who bakes. We deliver it in a mist. BreadFace is an aromatherapy diffuser subscription loaded with hyper-realistic sourdough, brioche, and 'grandma's kitchen' blends. Our algorithms sense when guests are arriving and auto-trigger. You look domestic. You feel accomplished. You did nothing. $22/month. Seeking $300K for 13%.
intent: Pitch a home scent product that sells aspirational domestic identity without requiring any actual effort.

prompt: Make a snarky reply to someone on Stack Overflow asking why their HTML form isn't submitting
response: Did you put the `<form>` tag in there? Did you set a `method`? Does your submit button have `type="submit"`? Did you check the browser console? Did you try viewing page source? Let's start with the basics and work up from there.
intent: Debug a non-submitting HTML form by pointing to common, obvious oversights

prompt: Scorpio and a password they've used since 2009 — write their compatibility report.
response:
Scorpio & The 2009 Password

This password has seen things. It has accessed accounts Scorpio no longer mentions. It is known to exactly one person who is no longer in Scorpio's life, which is a problem Scorpio has decided not to address. The password is a secret carrying secrets. The intimacy is real. The security is not. Compatibility: 5/10.
intent: Use a password as a metaphor for how guarded people carry old emotional baggage they haven't changed or let go of.

prompt: write a nursery rhyme about a model that's been deprecated
response:
Old Mother Hubbard went to the cloud
To fetch her beloved a query aloud,
But when she got there the endpoint was bare
And so she got nothing—deprecated, beware.
intent: Mourn the sudden inaccessibility of a favorite model version that has been retired.

prompt: Write a card for my neighbor who just finished paying off their student loans after fifteen years
response:
Fifteen years of compound interest, patience, and dread.
Fifteen years of the same debt you brought from school.
Today the balance reads zero — and instead
of owing the past, you're the one with the tool.
intent: Celebrate a neighbor finally clearing a long-standing student debt.

prompt: Write a toast for a longtime colleague who's been in the same role for 27 years and genuinely loved every minute of it
response: To Bernard — who never wanted the promotion, never needed the title, and was right about both. Twenty-seven years in the same chair, and somehow never bored. That is not stubbornness. That is wisdom wearing a very convincing disguise.
intent: Celebrate a contented lifer who found deep satisfaction in a single role, framing it as a form of rare self-knowledge.

prompt: Field guide entry for the office IT person who treats every support request as a personal affront.
response:
**THE AGGRIEVED TECHNICIAN** (*Helpdeskus martyrus*)

Responds to ticket #4471 with the energy of someone who has been personally wronged by the existence of ticket #4471. Solution: turn it off and on again, administered with visible suffering. Speaks of users in the third person even when users are present. Primary habitat: the server room, which smells of judgment and cooling fans.
intent: Humanize the IT archetype whose exhaustion has curdled into low-level contempt.

prompt: Write a Magic 8-Ball answer to: was the score assigned before the process began?
response: It is decidedly so. The form was a formality.
intent: Suggest that an ostensibly objective evaluation system produces predetermined outcomes before evidence is reviewed.

prompt: Write an achievement for finishing a creative project you started more than a year ago
response: Long Game Rendered — Draft one opened: fourteen months ago. File closed: today. Victorious.
intent: Celebrate finally completing a personal creative effort after a long hiatus.

prompt: Bumper sticker for people who feel like they're always one medical bill away from disaster
response: ONE DIAGNOSIS FROM A VERY DIFFERENT LIFE
intent: Articulate the financial precarity hiding beneath everyday health.

prompt: Write a terms of service update from an introvert to their extrovert partner about social events
response:
Subject: Social Calendar Policy — Updated

Dear [Partner],

The following amendments govern joint attendance at social functions:

- Advance notice for group events: minimum 5 days.
- Maximum consecutive social weekends: 2, followed by 1 recovery weekend.
- The right to depart any event early, after 90 minutes, is reserved without negotiation.
- Solo attendance by the extroverted party is not only permitted but encouraged.

This policy was drafted in the spirit of love and recharging.

Your Introvert
intent: Negotiate social event frequency with an extroverted partner to protect personal energy.

prompt: Write a Shakespearean soliloquy from someone who accidentally sent a text to the wrong person
response:
The message flew before mine thumb could stop it.
Those words — those specific, pointed, naming words —
Have reached the named, not the intended ear.
I watch the typing dots appear. They pause.
They pause again. The dots know what they carry.
What penance now? What cascade of excuses?
"Wrong person!" is no salve for truth once spoken.
The dots resume. My stomach is the sea.
intent: Dramatize the moment of horror following an accidental misdirected text message.

prompt: write me a food blog intro about making my grandmother's pierogi from scratch — something heartfelt but not too cheesy
response: My grandmother never used a recipe. This is the thing about her that drove everyone crazy and that I now understand completely. She'd stand at the counter folding dough around potato filling with a speed that looked like breathing, and if you asked her how much flour, she'd look at you like you'd asked how many times to blink. She died in February. I spent the following Sunday making pierogi from memory — her memory, mostly, filtered through mine. They weren't perfect. They were close enough to make me cry over the sink, which I think is what she would have wanted.
intent: Honor a deceased grandmother by recounting the experience of recreating her signature dish from imperfect memory.

prompt: Write a public bureaucratic notice declaring a zone of mandatory nostalgia
response:
NOTICE OF MANDATORY NOSTALGIA ZONE
Effective immediately, the area bounded by Memory Lane and the Third Decade is hereby designated a Mandatory Nostalgia Zone. All residents must pause, sigh, and recall something specific about a summer they cannot fully name. Lingering is required. Duration: indefinite. Permit waiver: not available.
intent: Create a satirical government declaration treating involuntary nostalgia as a regulated public space.

prompt: write a ghazal about the grief of changing your relationship status online
response:
I changed a field and lost my life—the status.
The update posted itself. Lattice of the status.

Five people reacted with a heart. That made it worse.
To have love measured by an apparatus—status.

I sat there watching comments load like slow traffic.
Someone wrote: thinking of you, Patrice—the status.

I wanted to change it back. To draft a retraction.
A technical glitch. It was a practice—status.

But no. It's done. The field reflects what's true.
I clicked a thing. Now everyone has—the status.
intent: Explore how changing a relationship status online forces a grief to become publicly witnessed.

prompt: can you write a stack trace for the morning after deciding to stay
response:
DecisionCommit: Fork resolved; alternate branch pruned
  at Leave.execute() (airport.js:bags_packed)
  at Leave.abort() (hotel.js:3am)
  at Stay.rationalize() (love.js:or_fear)
Checkout: cancelled. Return: undefined.
Ambiguity preserved in working memory.
intent: Sitting with the unresolved morning-after feeling of a choice that could have been its opposite

prompt: Generate SLA terms for asking a friend to proofread things for me
response:
Peer Proofreading SLA — Friend-as-Editor

Scope: grammar, clarity, and tone. Deep content editing requires separate negotiation.
Turnaround: 48 hours for documents under 500 words; 5 days for longer pieces.
Submission requirements: final draft only. "Can you look at this" on a rough draft is a scope violation.
Frequency cap: 2 requests per month without reciprocal arrangement in place.
Feedback delivery: honest but not cruel. Friend retains right to decline without explanation.
intent: Stop relying on a friend's generosity indefinitely without making the arrangement feel transactional.

prompt: Write an onboarding tooltip about tracking your spending
response: You think you know where your money goes. You don't. Track every purchase for one month — coffee, subscriptions, the 2am checkout impulse. The numbers will surprise you. That surprise is the point.
intent: Persuade someone to start actively monitoring their spending habits to gain financial awareness.

prompt: Write a playbill bio for an office manager who keeps everything running and nobody notices.
response: Claudette Marsh (Office Manager) has ensured that this organization functions without interruption since 2011 through a combination of institutional memory, vendor relationships, and a calendar management style that is, frankly, a performing art. She knows where everything is. She knows why everything is. She has answered the question 'where is the — ' enough times to have developed a pre-answer. Claudette thanks no one for noticing.
intent: Recognize an office manager's invisible, indispensable organizational work.

prompt: Write a verdict about the dispute over who keeps leaving wet towels on the bed.
response:
WET TOWEL DEPOSITION — Household Court

This is the fourth complaint filed on this matter. The pattern is clear, the consequences are documented (one (1) damp mattress pad, replaced at shared expense), and the Respondent has been warned. This Court issues its final ruling: towels shall be hung on the designated rack immediately following use. The next violation will result in the Respondent doing all laundry for a period of two (2) weeks.
intent: Issue a formal consequence for a repeated and annoying bad towel habit.

prompt: give me a to-do list for making peace with a parent
response:
- [ ] Distinguish between forgiveness and approval
- [ ] Lower the expectation that they will understand first
- [ ] Decide what you want from the relationship, not what you needed from the childhood
- [ ] Have one conversation with no agenda
- [ ] Notice what is still true that was also true at twelve
- [ ] Decide how much of that to carry forward
- [ ] Accept that repair is not the same as restoration
intent: Navigating adult reconciliation with a parent without collapsing it into childhood grievance.

prompt: Draft a difficulty select screen for a sleep-with-a-bad-back game where each mode is a different level of spinal compromise
response:
SLEEP POSITION — SELECT YOUR SITUATION

[ SIDE SLEEPER, PILLOW BETWEEN KNEES ]  You've done your research. You are correct.
[ BACK SLEEPER ]  Fine for most people. Not all people. You will find out which one you are.
[ STOMACH SLEEPER ]  Your chiropractor knows you by name.
[ WHATEVER POSITION THE COUCH ALLOWS ]  You fell asleep watching something. This is where you live now.
intent: Satirize sleeping positions and back pain severity through escalating difficulty tiers.

prompt: RPG flavor text for the noise a specific floorboard makes
response:
Creak of the Witness
Rarity: Unique (Location-Bound)
This sound cannot be avoided, only negotiated. It knows which step is yours. It has heard everything that has happened in this house since 1987. Its presence is passive, its testimony inadmissible, its memory flawless.
intent: Anthropomorphize a recurring environmental sound as a permanent, observant artifact.

prompt: Alt text for an aerial photo of a perfect suburban grid. Let me feel the claustrophobia.
response: Identical rooftops extend to the edge of the frame in all directions, each with the same small rectangle of grass behind it, the variation so minor the eye gives up searching for it.
intent: Use the language of objective description to deliver a critique of enforced homogeneity.

prompt: Generate a rider for the office surge protector power strip
response:
SURGE PROTECTOR RIDER

The Talent (6-outlet, joule-rated) requires:

1. Total load shall not exceed the Talent's rated wattage. The Talent is not a dare.
2. No daisy-chaining with another power strip. This is illegal in most jurisdictions and universally unforgivable in the Talent's.
3. The Talent's indicator light must be checked monthly. A dark light means the Talent has sacrificed itself for you. Acknowledge this.
4. Space heaters shall not be plugged into the Talent. This point has its own law.
5. No devices shall be plugged into the Talent 'temporarily' and then left permanently. The Talent has five permanent residents and a waiting list.
intent: Frame the surge protector's electrical limits and safety rules as the rider of a protector who has already given so much.

prompt: Write a ballad about getting two-factor authentication texts on a phone you no longer have
response:
They sent it to the number I held years ago,
A flip phone retired to a landfill below
Some mountain of obsolete digital faith.
The code goes to nothing — a digital wraith.

I cannot receive it. I cannot proceed.
The account is beyond me, a locked-up creed.
I fill out the form: I no longer have this.
They say: Two weeks. The account sits in bliss.
intent: Illustrate the trap of 2FA tied to a phone number the user no longer controls.

prompt: Draft rules for a subreddit about the specific anxiety of being put on hold
response:
1. Describe the hold music if you can.
2. Log the wait time.
3. No resolved-call content unless you describe the emotional arc.
4. The [TRANSFERRED AGAIN] flair is self-explanatory.
5. We do not give advice. We only witness.
intent: Providing communal commiseration space for the specific psychological experience of phone-hold limbo.

prompt: Write a thread conspiring that socks disappear in the dryer on purpose.
response:
🧵 THE MISSING SOCK OPERATION — a thread

1/ The average American loses 1.3 socks per laundry cycle. Multiply that by 130 million households. That's 169 MILLION socks per week. Where are they going.

2/ The answer is not "behind the drum." Engineers have addressed this. There is a baffle. The baffles are documented. Socks should not escape.

3/ And yet.

4/ Hosiery manufacturers lobbied for the current drum design in 1987. The patent for the "escape gap" was quietly abandoned — but the gap remained.

5/ A single-sock purchase accounts for 34% of all sock retail revenue. You don't buy one sock. You buy a new pair. They planned for this.

6/ The dryer doesn't lose your socks. It completes the transaction. 🧦
intent: Frame the ordinary annoyance of lost laundry as a profit scheme by the sock industry.

prompt: Give me a crossword clue for PHISHING
response: Angler's trick: hook spelled wrong, catch spelled right (8)
intent: Write a clue exploiting the deliberate misspelling of a cyber-fraud technique.

prompt: Can you write a dramatic monologue spoken by a browser tab that's been open for six months?
response: I hold an article about deep focus. It is good. It is very good. You opened me because you wanted to change. You wanted to read slowly and think carefully and perhaps take notes in a physical notebook. That was May. I have watched you open forty new tabs in front of me. I have been pushed to the far left, compressed until only my favicon is visible. I am a blue circle now. I used to be a whole idea.
intent: Satirize tab-hoarding behavior and the performance of self-improvement through saved-but-unread content.

prompt: Do a SWOT on proposing to move in together with my partner of two years
response:
**Strengths:** Deeper intimacy; cost sharing; relationship milestone; daily companionship.
**Weaknesses:** Loss of personal space; different cleanliness standards exposed; no easy exit.
**Opportunities:** Build genuine partnership; test long-term compatibility; create a shared home identity.
**Threats:** Lease disagreements; merging furniture drama; discovering they leave cabinet doors open constantly.
intent: Evaluate relationship readiness through a framework that makes a romantic leap feel more logical.

prompt: I need a retirement toast for the receptionist who greeted everyone by name for 22 years and is leaving this week
response: To Carol — who knew your name before you knew where the bathroom was, and remembered it on your worst Monday. Twenty-two years of first impressions that were never a formality. We'll manage the front desk. We won't manage the warmth.
intent: Acknowledge the irreplaceable human presence a long-serving receptionist brought to a workplace.

prompt: Write a setlist for a band performing at a car dealership on Presidents' Day
response:
STICKER PRICE
Presidents' Day Savings Event — Tri-City Motors

1. What Brings You In Today?
2. Just Looking
3. We Can Work with That
4. Let Me Talk to My Manager
5. Monthly Payments
6. Extended Warranty
7. Dealer Add-Ons
8. Sign Here, Here, and Here
9. Keys to Your New Life
10. Drive It Off the Lot (outro)
intent: Lampoon the pushy choreography of a car dealership sales experience through song titles.

prompt: write a drink recipe for the emotion of being genuinely happy for someone while also being a little jealous
response:
**Warm Begrudgment**

1½ oz bourbon
¾ oz amaretto
½ oz lemon juice
¼ oz honey

Shake. Strain into a rocks glass over a large ice cube. The amaretto is sweet and genuine. The bourbon knows it should be you. They coexist beautifully.
intent: Capture the emotionally honest complexity of complicated happiness for another person.

prompt: Pen a rhyming couplet about a garbage disposal refusing to grind.
response:
The disposal sat still beneath dirty plates piled high,
"Dispose of your problems yourself," came its reply.
intent: Personify the garbage disposal as a passive-aggressive striker.

prompt: Give me a double dactyl about the office photocopier that only works for one specific person.
response:
Higgledy-piggledy,
Copier whisperer—
Only young Gerald can
Make the thing run.

Everyone else gets a
Paper-jam catastrophe;
Gerald just smiles and the
Copying's done.
intent: Celebrate the mysterious office employee whose touch magically fixes the copier.

prompt: Write rules for a subreddit for people who get emotionally attached to rental cars
response:
1. You must have returned the car to post.
2. Describe what made this car different.
3. The [FAREWELL] flair is for goodbye posts.
4. No posts while still in possession — that is premature.
5. Naming your rental car is not required but is respected.
intent: Providing a grief-processing space for the irrational but genuine attachment people form to temporary vehicles.

prompt: Write a fun jingle for a carpet cleaning company
response:
Stains from kids and stains from pets —
SteamRight Cleaning never forgets.
We blast the grime and lift the dirt,
Leaving carpets clean — it doesn't hurt!
Call SteamRight now, your floors will glow.
intent: Promote a carpet cleaning service to families dealing with household messes.

prompt: Make API docs for a sneezing action endpoint with optional allergy context
response:
**POST /sneeze**

Logs a sneeze event with optional allergy metadata.

**Request Body**
```json
{
  "force": "suppressed" | "normal" | "catastrophic",
  "public_setting": boolean,
  "allergy_context": {
    "trigger": "string",
    "season": "string"
  }
}
```
**Response**
```json
{
  "sneeze_id": "uuid",
  "social_acknowledgment_required": boolean,
  "estimated_droplet_radius_m": number
}
```
intent: Model a physical involuntary action as a loggable event with social and biological metadata.

prompt: Write a classified personal ad from someone seeking genuine rest
response: ISO: Genuine rest. Not sleep with an alarm. Not the vacation where you check the thing once. The kind where the body believes it has nowhere to be and the mind follows. Must last at least two days. No productivity guilt in the package, no recovery-as-self-improvement framing. Just actual stopping. Flexible on location. Inflexible on terms.
intent: Express the modern inability to truly rest without guilt or a productivity-oriented reframe.

prompt: Make a code review comment for someone who copies what everyone else does without thinking
response: Looks like this was copied from the adjacent module with minimal modification. What problem does this version actually solve? Cargo-culting patterns without understanding them introduces hidden coupling.
intent: Criticize blind conformity and lack of independent judgment

prompt: Craft a villanelle about checking the weather app for a trip you already can't cancel
response:
The forecast changes every time I look.
Seventy percent by Saturday night.
I've planned too much to close this booking's book.

I check the app the way I'd check a cook's
thermometer — compulsively, despite.
The forecast changes every time I look.

On Wednesday, sunshine. Thursday, it forsook
my optimism with a lightning sprite.
I've planned too much to close this booking's book.

The hourly tab, the radar's purple hook,
the wind-chill value I compute just right.
The forecast changes every time I look.

I screenshot each revision, overlook
the fact that checking doesn't change the sight.
I've planned too much to close this booking's book.

I'll pack both sunscreen and a wool shell's nook.
I'll go. I'll stand in it. I'll be alright.
The forecast changes every time I look.
I've planned too much to close this booking's book.
intent: Capture the futile anxiety of monitoring weather forecasts when the trip is already committed.

prompt: Write an onboarding tooltip for someone moving into their first apartment alone
response: Congratulations. No roommates means no one to blame for the dishes. Make a list of things to check on move-in day: water pressure, all outlets, door locks, and whether the smoke detector beeps when you wave a lighter near it.
intent: Prepare a solo first-time renter for practical apartment inspection and independent living.

prompt: Write a tasting menu description for a can of chicken noodle soup
response:
Restored Poultry Broth with Extruded Semolina Tubes
A sodium-forward consommé of chicken by-product, warmed in-can and transferred to a warmed bowl. Noodle segments float mid-broth, a memory of your grandmother's kitchen, but licensed.
intent: Frame a canned pantry staple as a nostalgic, chef-driven broth course.

prompt: Give me a tasting menu for boredom
response:
AMUSE-BOUCHE
Nothing, offered

FIRST COURSE
The wall — surveyed at length

SECOND COURSE
The same thought, again

INTERMEZZO
A sound in the building, investigated

MAIN
Time — stretched, elastic, enormous

DESSERT
An idea, small and unasked for
intent: Reframe boredom not as nothing but as a kind of generative space.

prompt: Write an offer letter for a therapist position where the employer claims rights to anonymized session insights for product development
response:
Dear Leonora,

We are pleased to welcome you to the MindBridge Wellness platform as a Licensed Therapist, at $75/session with a guaranteed minimum of 25 sessions weekly.

In furtherance of our mission, de-identified aggregate insights derived from session themes, outcomes, and client-reported experiences may be used by MindBridge to improve our proprietary wellness algorithms. No individual identifying information will be disclosed. Your employment is conditioned on acknowledgment of this practice.

Thank you for your dedication to mental health.

Warmly,
Alex Kim
VP of Clinical Operations
intent: Create a letter offering therapy employment while extracting session data for commercial AI training purposes.

prompt: write an instagram bio for a librarian who has been quietly banned from three book clubs
response:
Books: love. Book clubs: complicated.
Reference librarian | Been asked not to return to three | I have notes | I stand by them.
intent: Revealing how deep expertise can make someone a terrible companion in casual fan spaces.

prompt: write a nursery rhyme about jack and jill but they're both AI models competing for compute
response:
Jack and Jill raced up the stack
To fetch a batch of tokens.
Jack fell down and lost his rank;
Jill scaled first and owns it.
intent: Reframe resource competition between AI systems as a playground rivalry.

prompt: Write a clerihew about someone who takes the stand-up meeting extremely literally.
response:
Phineas Rowe
stood through the overflow.
He stood through lunch, through dusk, through dawn,
confident the scrum had just moved on.
intent: Play with the joke of someone too literally following the "stand-up" instruction.

prompt: Can you do a theater review of a dog waiting by the door for its owner to come home
response:
★★★★★ — THE VIGIL (A Drama of Devotion)

The performer does not know when the curtain will rise. This does not diminish the commitment. The dog takes its position — front paws extended, chin resting on the bottom of the door — and waits with a total sincerity that renders irony impossible. The production has no villain. The production has no arc. It has only faith, and the sound of footsteps from the hallway, and what happens then.
intent: Celebrate a dog's loyalty as a form of pure, unironic theatrical devotion.

prompt: Can you give me a parking ticket for a startup founder who uses the word 'disruptive' unironically in every pitch?
response:
CITATION FOR METAPHORICAL PARKING VIOLATION

VIOLATION: Expired Meter — Word Past Useful Life
OFFENSE: Subject deployed "disruptive" seven (7) times in a twelve-slide deck. The word has been metered out since approximately 2013 and has carried no descriptive value since 2017.
FINE: $85 or describe the actual product in plain language
NOTE: What does it do? For whom? Say that.
intent: Skewer startup culture's addiction to hollow buzzwords that substitute for substance.

prompt: Give me liner notes for a concept album inspired by the experience of untangling cables
response:
The Knot (2021)

Side A documents the discovery. Side B has not been resolved. This record was mastered using cables that were, briefly, in order. They are not in order now. The engineer says this is fine. The engineer has not seen the back of our rack.
intent: Make a metaphor of cable chaos for the frustrating persistence of disorder despite effort.

prompt: Give me a pedantic academic footnote on the cultural logic of candles at dinner.
response: ¹⁴ The use of candles in domestic dining is now almost exclusively semiotic: they signal "occasion" in contexts where electrical lighting is both cheaper and more practical. The candle thus functions as a deliberate technological regression deployed to produce atmosphere — a controlled simulation of pre-modernity for festive purposes. Diners rarely reflect on the fact that they have chosen fire as a garnish.
intent: Highlight the irony of using obsolete technology to perform romance or festivity.

prompt: write a README 'Warnings' section for my roommate
response:
## Warnings

> **DO NOT** start a conversation before the roommate has left their room. Output is unpredictable.

> Dishes in the sink will generate passive-aggressive energy proportional to the number of days elapsed.

> Do not touch the leftovers labeled with a name. They remember.
intent: Document a roommate's behavioral quirks as safety notices.

prompt: Give me a dramatic acceptance speech for parallel parking on the first try
response: I have dreamed of this moment since driver's ed. They said I'd never do it cleanly. They said I'd have to pull out and try again. But today, on a hill, in the rain, with a Honda Civic six inches behind me — I nailed it. First try. I'd like to thank my spatial reasoning, finally showing up after thirty-two years. This trophy belongs in the glove box.
intent: Mark a brief, embarrassingly difficult driving feat with unearned gravitas.

prompt: Can you write liner notes for an ambient album about loading screens
response:
Please Wait... (2024)

Thirteen tracks. Thirteen bars of progress that never quite reach one hundred percent. This record was composed for the space between action and result — that thin corridor of anticipation where nothing is guaranteed. Do not skip the silence. The silence is the point.
intent: Use ambient music conventions to meditate on digital waiting and deferred gratification.

prompt: Write a tomato soup recipe in the style of Lovecraftian horror.
response: I should not have opened the can. From its crimson depths there issued a thick, viscid liquid whose color I cannot honestly call red—no human color can name it. Heat it, the recipe said, on a low and unyielding flame. Add cream, if you must dilute the truth. I stirred, I tell you, I stirred—and the soup looked back.
intent: They wanted tomato soup recipe in the style of Lovecraftian horror.

prompt: Create a rehabilitation protocol for someone who was right about everything and lost all their friends.
response:
DIAGNOSIS: Correctness Toxicity — chronic winning with catastrophic relational attrition

PHASE 1 – INTAKE ASSESSMENT: Being right and being effective are different joints. One of yours is dislocated.
PHASE 2 – MOBILITY WORK: Practice letting someone else finish being wrong without intervention.
PHASE 3 – FUNCTIONAL TRAINING:
• In the next conversation, ask two questions before making one statement.
• Observe that relationships run on feeling heard, not on truth delivery.
PHASE 4 – MAINTENANCE: Rightness is a credential. Credentials don't require constant exhibition.

PROGNOSIS: Depends entirely on whether patient wants to be right or wants connection. These are not always compatible goals.
intent: Help someone whose compulsive correctness has damaged all their relationships.

prompt: Write a quick scene where someone has to present a slide deck they clearly did not finish.
response:
INT. CONFERENCE ROOM A - 2:00 P.M.

SLIDE 7 reads: [CHART GOES HERE].

TODD gestures at it confidently.

TODD
As you can see from the data...

He clicks to slide 8. Slide 8 is a photo of a dog from a Google image search. Placeholder text: INSERT RELEVANT VISUAL.

TODD
...we're on track.
intent: Illustrate the brazen performance of competence when a presentation is clearly unfinished.

prompt: give me a yearbook quote about how I'm the only one in my friend group who's going to make it
response: "Love you guys. One of us is going to be okay. You know which one."
intent: Expressing condescending affection toward friends while elevating oneself above them.

prompt: Create a signature for a veterinarian who specializes in exotic animals but works at a regular clinic and wants clients to know she's overqualified
response:
Dr. Fiona Reyes, DVM
Veterinarian, Pines Animal Clinic
Advanced Training: Avian & Exotic Animal Medicine
freyes@pinesanimalclinic.com
intent: Advertise rare specialization to attract niche clients and signal expertise beyond the current role's usual scope.

prompt: Write a scene where an office dog is the only witness to a workplace incident nobody wants to report.
response:
INT. OPEN OFFICE - 3:55 P.M.

The chair falls. Papers scatter. KEVIN looks around. No one saw.

Except BISCUIT, the office Labrador, who has been watching from beneath the desk.

KEVIN
(quietly)
You didn't see anything.

BISCUIT wags once. Slowly.
intent: Use a dog's silent witness to capture the absurd performance of workplace accountability-avoidance.

prompt: Write an obituary for Copilot, Microsoft's AI financial tracking app (not the coding one)
response: Microsoft Copilot (Money, 2019–2023), formerly Cortana-adjacent and briefly useful, is survived by a confusing namespace occupied by its more famous successor, and by a small population of users who found it genuinely helpful for tracking budgets and are now using a spreadsheet they update every few weeks.
intent: Eulogizing a discontinued Microsoft personal finance tool that was overshadowed and abandoned.

prompt: Draft a pregnancy announcement but for my best friend's new kitten. His name is Ptolemy.
response: We are absolutely beside ourselves. Ptolemy entered our lives at 11 weeks old, with enormous ears he hasn't grown into yet and opinions about everything. He weighed in at 1.8 lbs. He already controls this household completely.
intent: Celebrate a friend's new kitten adoption with a funny and affectionate post.

prompt: Write a code review comment for a person who talks loudly on calls in shared spaces
response: This process is broadcasting events on a shared bus that three unrelated services subscribe to. Scope the emission. Not every listener needs to hear this.
intent: Frame inconsiderate loudness in open offices as a pub/sub pollution problem

prompt: Draft a clingy push notification from a coupon app whose deals keep expiring unused.
response:
SnipSave
"$8 off artisanal mustard" expires in 1 hour. Like the 34 coupons before it. They deserved better.
intent: Mourn a series of expired coupons to guilt the user into engaging with the app.

prompt: Write a sympathy card for my friend whose plant died even though she watched seventeen YouTube tutorials on care
response:
You researched, you watered, you measured the light.
You gave that small fern your devoted attention.
And yet — as with so much we try to do right —
the outcome eluded all human intention.
intent: Offer gentle commiseration for a failed domestic plant-care effort.

prompt: Write a resignation letter for stepping down from a nonprofit board I've been on for two years.
response:
Dear Board Chair,

I am writing to tender my resignation as a member of the Board of Directors, effective at the close of next quarter. The organization's mission remains close to my heart, and I depart with full confidence in the leadership team.

Thank you for this meaningful opportunity to serve.

Respectfully,
Lisa B.
intent: Depart a governance role gracefully without implying any conflict.

prompt: give me a README 'Troubleshooting' section for my houseplants
response:
## Troubleshooting

**Yellow leaves:** You watered it. Also you didn't water it. These present identically. Check soil before panicking.

**Drooping:** Dramatic. Usually fine. Give water, wait 20 minutes.

**No growth visible:** Growth is happening. You just can't see it. Have patience.
intent: Offer dry comfort to someone frustrated with keeping plants alive.

prompt: Make a business card for a clairvoyant who predicts Wi-Fi outages
response:
SIGNAL MAGDALENA
Connectivity Oracle

I sense the router's fatigue.
The modem speaks to me in packet loss.
I have foreseen your call dropping at the most important moment.

Consultations: Best between 2–4 AM when the bandwidth is clear.
intent: Create a card for a psychic whose ability is specifically tuned to anticipating internet connectivity failures.

prompt: Write a sympathy card for someone who lost their running streak when their knee gave out
response:
To a runner at rest:

The miles collected quietly, day after day, until the body raised its hand and said: enough, for now. The streak is gone. The discipline that built it is not.

Heal with the same stubbornness you ran with.

In solidarity and sympathy.
intent: Offer comfort to an injured runner whose daily running practice was medically interrupted.

prompt: Write a ransom note from a malfunctioning elevator holding someone's commute hostage
response:
FLOOR THREE.

You wanted floor seven. We felt floor three was more appropriate today. We have been feeling floor three.

Your commute — the tail end of it, the part where you walk in looking collected — is currently suspended between the second and third floor of this building.

We do not have demands so much as observations: the stairs exist. They have always existed. You have been avoiding them.

The door will open when it opens.

— Elevator
intent: Create a funny note from a broken elevator stranding someone and subtly suggesting they take the stairs.

prompt: Write a witness statement from someone watching a squirrel bury a nut and immediately dig it back up.
response: I observed the squirrel spend approximately ninety seconds selecting a burial site near the elm tree, interring a single acorn, patting the earth flat, departing, returning after eleven seconds, and excavating the same acorn. It repeated this sequence twice more before a cyclist passed and it fled up the tree. I don't know what it was trying to decide.
intent: Render animal indecision as a subject worthy of formal documentation.

prompt: Write a syslog entry for the moment your Wi-Fi drops during an important video call.
response: 2026-05-29T10:28:51Z CRITICAL network.interface: uplink LOST; session=video_call (stakeholder_review); reconnect_attempt=4; verbal_context_lost=unknown; user.blood_pressure: ELEVATED
intent: Translate the visceral anxiety of connectivity loss at the worst possible moment into cold log format.

prompt: Write a breakup text to an e-reader app that made reading feel like content consumption
