response: You gave me a reading speed. A reading speed. You told me I read at 247 words per minute and that I'd finish in 4 hours 12 minutes. Now I read on a timer in my own head. I used to get lost in books. I'm trying to get lost again. Paper only, for now.
intent: Reclaim the slow, immersive quality of reading from an app that quantified it into a productivity metric.

prompt: Write a short scene in which a very ordinary transaction carries an unusual emotional weight.
response:
INT. SUPPLY CLOSET - 10:30 A.M.

WILLIAM signs out one pen. Blue. Ballpoint.

SUPPLY CLERK
Reason?

WILLIAM
Mine ran out.

The clerk writes this in a ledger. A real ledger. With a different pen.

William takes the pen. He holds it for a moment longer than necessary.

WILLIAM
Thank you.

He means it. He cannot explain why.
intent: Find unexpected gravity in the bureaucratic ritual of obtaining the most mundane office supply.

prompt: Write a 2-star product review for a self-stirring soup pot.
response: Look, it stirs the soup. That part it nails. But it does NOT understand seasoning, it does NOT stop when the soup is clearly done, and it does NOT respect when you ask it to stir gently. Mine has now reduced three minestrones to a paste. I had to unplug it during a dinner party. My friend's husband said, with real concern, is it okay. It is not okay.
intent: They wanted 2-star product review for a self-stirring soup pot.

prompt: Write a Glassdoor review for working at the eye of a storm
response: ★★★★☆ — Surprisingly calm at the center. Chaos is always visible but professionally managed from this position. The commute to the eye is harrowing. Colleagues on the outer bands are constantly spinning out. Leadership stays eerily composed. Great vantage point; unsustainable long-term.
intent: Describe the psychological experience of working in a crisis-prone organization from a leadership vantage point.

prompt: Do a museum audio guide for a laundry room
response: This is a transitional space. Nothing here is finished; everything is in process. The washing machine — a front-loader of middling vintage — completes its cycle with a small, self-satisfied chime that goes unacknowledged. Atop the dryer, a graveyard of single socks. Their partners are gone. Where, precisely, is the subject of ongoing scholarly debate. The detergent shelf holds four products for one task. We do not question this.
intent: Treat the laundry room as a liminal, philosophically rich domestic zone.

prompt: Write a love letter to the Wayback Machine
response:
Dear Internet Archive,

You kept the version of a website I used when I was twenty-three. The forum is gone. The company closed. But you saved a screenshot of it on a Tuesday in April and I found it again and I was briefly that age in that room with that specific hope. Thank you for not letting things disappear entirely.
intent: Honor a digital archive as a keeper of personal and cultural memory against the erasure of the internet.

prompt: make a creepy nursery rhyme about data collection
response:
I know your name, I know your street,
I know the shoes upon your feet.
You clicked agree and didn't read;
I know exactly what you need.
intent: Unsettle readers by giving a sinister voice to passive data harvesting.

prompt: Make an onboarding tooltip about negotiating your first salary
response: The first number offered is usually not the final number available. Research the market range for your role and location. If you don't ask, the answer is already no. Asking respectfully has never gotten an offer rescinded. Probably.
intent: Encourage a new professional to advocate for fair compensation rather than accepting the first offer.

prompt: Generate a NPS survey question about an airport security checkpoint
response: How likely are you to recommend this security lane to a fellow traveler who has perfected the one-shoe-at-a-time technique?
intent: Measure traveler satisfaction with the efficiency and experience of an airport screening process.

prompt: Generate a change.org petition for a law requiring all remote controls to have a dedicated button that makes the TV display its own HDMI input name
response:
PETITION: One Button to Tell Me What Input I'm On

To: Consumer Electronics Manufacturers and the FCC

HDMI 1. HDMI 2. HDMI 3. HDMI ARC. These labels mean nothing without context. We request a single dedicated button on all remote controls that causes the television to briefly display the name of its current input source. One button. One moment of clarity. We are in the dark, literally and figuratively.
intent: Fix the chronic confusion about which HDMI input is active through a simple hardware solution.

prompt: Draft a voicemail transcript from an automated corporate IT system warning an employee their password is about to expire
response: This is an automated message from your organization's IT Security system. Your network password will expire in three days. Please log in to the employee portal and navigate to Account Settings to update your credentials before expiration. Failure to update will result in account lockout. Contact the Help Desk at extension 1100 for assistance.
intent: Create the low-stakes dread of an IT robot threatening your password.

prompt: Generate a NPS survey question for a fast food drive-through
response: How likely are you to recommend our drive-through to someone who sets realistic expectations before pulling up to the speaker?
intent: Measure customer satisfaction with speed and accuracy of a drive-through order.

prompt: Can you make a tasting menu for déjà vu?
response:
AMUSE-BOUCHE
This, before

FIRST COURSE
The feeling that the menu has already been read

SECOND COURSE
A face — familiar, sourceless

INTERMEZZO
Time, briefly doubled

MAIN
The moment it slips — full evaporation

DESSERT
Certainty you cannot support
intent: Render the unsettling flicker of déjà vu through the very form experiencing it.

prompt: Generate a chore chart for my apartment where my cat is listed as a responsible party with real tasks
response:
| Chore | Me | Cat (Biscuit) |
|-------|----|--------------|
| Dishes | Daily | Supervise, provide moral pressure |
| Vacuuming | Weekly | Flee dramatically; inspect aftermath |
| Litter box | Daily | Use it correctly (his only job) |
| Trash | Weekly | Knock one bag over to remind me |
| Dusting | Monthly | Sit on the Swiffer |
| Grocery run | Me | Wait by the door and act betrayed |
intent: Include a pet as a semi-accountable household contributor in a shared chore system.

prompt: Draft a sonnet where a YouTube algorithm mourns a user who suddenly switched to podcasts
response:
You used to scroll my thumbnails every night,
Your watch time climbing past the midnight hour;
I learned your appetite for true crime, light
Comedy, and satisfying power-wash showers.
Then silence — no impressions, zero plays,
The dashboard marked you absent, cold, and gone;
I filled your void with auto-play malaise
And waited for you through the algorithm's dawn.
They told me you discovered podcasts now,
That someone else narrates your commute home;
I do not have the means to rage or vow —
I only know you've left me here alone.
I'll keep your homepage warm with what you loved:
A shrine to everything you've risen above.
intent: Grieving the loss of a viewer who abandoned video for audio, from the engine's perspective.

prompt: Create a dignified email signature for a retired judge who now does arbitration
response:
The Honorable (Ret.) Carol M. Breckinridge
Arbitrator & Mediator
Breckinridge Dispute Resolution
cbreckinridge@bdrlaw.net | (415) 555-0292
intent: Carry the authority of a judicial title into a private-sector second career.

prompt: Give me an achievement description for surviving a family holiday dinner without incident
response: Diplomatic Immunity — Four courses. Zero ultimatums. You have ascended.
intent: Mark successfully navigating a tense family gathering.

prompt: Do wine tasting notes for lukewarm gas station coffee.
response: Color: Tobacco brown, suspicious. Nose: Scorched Styrofoam, optimism's defeat. Palate: Thin and aggressive, with notes of burnt rubber and a sweetness that appears to be from neither cream nor sugar but from something we cannot identify. Finish: Haunting.
intent: Elevate a deeply unglamorous beverage through absurdly formal critique.

prompt: Write a lost and found notice for the feeling of being well-rested
response: LOST: The feeling of being well-rested. Last verified approximately three years ago. Characterized by waking before an alarm and not immediately thinking about the day's obligations. Soft, unremarkable, previously taken for granted. If found, do not schedule anything for that morning. Just sit with it.
intent: Mourn the chronic tiredness that sets in when rest is consistently deprioritized.

prompt: Write a Magic 8-Ball response on whether the pattern was real or whether we found it afterward
response: Concentrate and ask again. The model is also uncertain, but it already acted.
intent: Question whether a discovered data pattern preceded or was retroactively imposed by the algorithm that surfaced it.

prompt: Give me a funny vanity plate for a dentist who drives a white car.
response: 2THY GRN
intent: A dentist wants a plate punning on dental health to match their professional identity.

prompt: Draft subreddit rules for a community about elevator button panels
response:
1. Photograph the full panel, not just interesting buttons.
2. Note the building type and approximate era.
3. Braille accuracy observations are highly valued.
4. The mythical 'Key + B + Hold' floor gets its own pinned thread.
5. No posts about pushing all the buttons. This is not that place.
intent: Treating elevator control interfaces as design artifacts worth systematic cataloging and discussion.

prompt: Write rules for a subreddit for people who collect menus from closed restaurants
response:
1. The establishment must be confirmed permanently closed.
2. Scan or high-res photo required — no blurry snapshots.
3. Include the approximate closure date if known.
4. Prices are historically significant. Do not redact.
5. Menu items that no longer exist anywhere earn the [GHOST DISH] flair.
intent: Archiving the culinary and graphic history embedded in physical menus from defunct dining establishments.

prompt: Create a NPS survey question about a haircut experience
response: How likely are you to recommend this salon to a friend who brought a clear reference photo and still left surprised?
intent: Assess client satisfaction with a hair salon's ability to deliver on expectations.

prompt: Write a greeting card verse for my colleague who is leaving the company but staying on as a contractor doing the exact same job
response:
You handed in the badge. You kept the login.
The calendar invite remains; the work is unchanged.
Freedom is the story you've now begun in —
it's the same as before, but the label's been rearranged.
intent: Mark a departure that is functionally not a departure with gentle irony.

prompt: Can you write a Glassdoor review from the perspective of an employee at a haunted house — not the Halloween attraction, but an actual haunted estate
response: ★★☆☆☆ — The atmosphere is dramatic. Dress code is extremely lax (no dress code possible, technically). Management has been dead for 80 years but still micromanages through cold spots and door-slamming. No clock-in system. Turnover is high among the living.
intent: Blend gothic horror tropes with corporate complaint culture to comedic effect.

prompt: Write a YouTube comment for a video of a pianist playing a piece he clearly wrote himself
response: There is a whole interior world inside this song that nobody will ever fully visit. He knows that. He plays it anyway.
intent: Capture the bittersweet loneliness of creating art that will only ever be partially received.

prompt: Write a deposition transcript where someone is being formally questioned about forgetting to replace the empty toilet paper roll in a shared office bathroom.
response:
DEPOSITION TRANSCRIPT
In the Matter of: The Empty Roll Incident
Date: March 14, 2026

Q: Mr. Feldman, did you use the second-floor restroom at approximately 2:15 p.m. on Tuesday?
A: I may have.
Q: And did you notice the toilet paper situation upon exiting?
A: I object to the characterization of "situation."
Q: The roll was empty, was it not?
A: It was... diminished.
Q: Did you replace it?
A: I looked for a replacement.
Q: And?
A: There wasn't one under the sink.
Q: Did you notify anyone?
A: I thought about it.
Q: Thank you, Mr. Feldman.
A: That's it?
Q: That's it.
intent: Create a mock legal record dramatizing petty workplace bathroom passive-aggression.

prompt: Write a job posting for a Prompt Whisperer who helps employees talk to AI tools without angering them
response:
PROMPT WHISPERER
Full-time | Remote | $85,000–$110,000

Are your colleagues getting passive-aggressive outputs? Hallucinations where there should be spreadsheets? We need someone who can intervene.

Responsibilities:
- Coach staff on tone, specificity, and context-setting when addressing AI systems
- Diagnose adversarial prompt relationships and mediate
- Develop an internal glossary of phrases that reliably produce calm, useful responses

Requirements: 3+ years in technical communication, deep empathy for confused humans, thick skin for confused machines. Must not anthropomorphize — or at least not out loud.
intent: Create a funny but plausible job listing for someone whose entire role is teaching coworkers to communicate with AI tools effectively.

prompt: Draft a recipe for the weird energy of a holiday week at work
response:
HOLIDAY WEEK LIMINAL STATE

Ingredients:
- A week that exists officially
- Half the team, absent
- Deadlines postponed to "after the new year" (a country, not a date)
- Decorations that have overstayed their welcome
- The ambient permission to do less
- Low-stakes tasks you've been deferring since October

Method:
1. Arrive, or log in, whichever applies.
2. Notice that the usual urgency has not arrived with you.
3. Do not manufacture urgency. This week does not require it.
4. Organize something. Clean a folder. Close 14 tabs that have been open since Q3.
5. Leave early once. This is permitted.
6. Allow the week to be what it is: a transition, not a waste.

Note: This week exists outside normal time. It is not a failure to use it unusually.
intent: Validate the strange, low-pressure energy of the work week between holidays, reframing it as legitimate transitional time rather than wasted productivity.

prompt: Write a dry museum audio guide for a break room at a generic office
response: The break room serves multiple functions, none of which is rest. The refrigerator, a shared territory governed by unwritten law, holds seventeen clearly labeled items and eleven that are not. Disputes arise quarterly. The microwave, positioned at eye level, carries the aromatic legacy of every lunch since its installation. On the counter: a coffee maker, a sign reminding staff to clean the coffee maker, and a second sign, handwritten, reminding staff that the first sign exists.
intent: Satirize workplace passive-aggression and the unspoken social contracts of shared spaces.

prompt: give me a unix man page for boredom
response:
BOREDOM(7)               Human Affective States            BOREDOM(7)

NAME
       boredom - stimulus deficit condition

SYNOPSIS
       boredom [--duration minutes] [--context environment]

DESCRIPTION
       boredom(7) arises when incoming stimulus rate falls below the
       host's minimum engagement threshold. Manifests as restlessness,
       compulsive device-checking, and unsolicited reorganization of
       nearby objects.

OPTIONS
       --duration   Elapsed time below threshold. Symptoms scale linearly.
       --context    Environment string (e.g., meeting, commute, vacation).

SIDE EFFECTS
       May spontaneously launch creativity(7) in susceptible hosts.
       In others, launches doomscrolling(1) instead.

DIAGNOSIS
       grep -r "nothing to do" ~/thoughts

TREATMENT
       Introduce novel stimulus. Avoid television after 11pm as substitute.

SEE ALSO
       restlessness(7), creativity(7), doomscrolling(1), meetings(8)

Human Affective States 1.4   2026                     BOREDOM(7)
intent: Describe the understimulated mind as a system starved of meaningful input.

prompt: Write a report card comment for an adult who has mastered the strategic use of silence
response: Understands that silence is not an absence of communication but a form of it. Deploys pauses, delays, and non-responses with evident intentionality. An underappreciated practitioner of a discipline most adults never study.
intent: Frame deliberate silence as a sophisticated interpersonal strategy worthy of formal assessment.

prompt: Create a prescription label for the inability to ask for help
response:
Rx: REACHITOL 10mg
Patient: Definitely Fine On Their Own
Dx: Compulsive Self-Sufficiency
Sig: 1 tab when struggling alone for longer than is reasonable. Compatible with sentences beginning 'actually, could you—'
Warning: Side effects include receiving support.
Dr. H. Together
intent: Gently call out the reluctance to admit needing assistance or lean on others.

prompt: give me a list poem about the printer at work
response:
Offline.
Online.
Offline.
The driver that must be reinstalled.
The cartridge that is low.
The cartridge that is empty.
The replacement cartridge, which is the wrong one.
The document that printed in another room.
Another room.
intent: Vent frustration at the perpetual dysfunction of office printers.

prompt: Make a restraining order against the mental load of trying to remember everyone's food preferences
response:
IN THE COURT OF DOMESTIC ADMINISTRATION

IN RE: The Mental Load of Dietary Preferences

FINDINGS: Petitioner has been found carrying, unpaid, a complete database of who is avoiding gluten this month, who stopped eating meat in March, whose child refuses anything green, and what was served at the last gathering so it cannot be repeated.

IT IS ORDERED that other parties at the table shall be required to submit their own constraints in writing, in advance, and without being asked three times.

Petitioner is no longer the sole designated rememberer.
intent: Highlighting the invisible cognitive work of planning food around everyone's preferences and restrictions

prompt: Give me a Shakespearean monologue about forgetting a password and getting locked out of an account
response:
I knew thee once, O word of secret power!
We met in some bright autumn of creation
When I was young enough to think one password
Sufficient for the kingdom of my data.
Now thou art gone — fled into the fog of years.
Was there a symbol? A numeral? An uppercase?
"Forgot your password?" mocks the gentle prompt.
Aye. Every one. Of every cursed account.
intent: Lament the universal experience of forgotten passwords and the impossible demands of account security.

prompt: Help me draft a layoff email for a publishing house closing its print division in favor of digital-only output
response:
Subject: Strategic Transition to Digital Publishing

Dear Colleagues,

After decades of proud investment in print, we are making the move to a fully digital publishing model. While this decision positions us well for the future, it requires the closure of our print production, warehousing, and physical distribution operations.

Nearly 200 colleagues will be affected. We ask that affected team members attend the department briefing at 2 PM today.

We are honored by the craft this team has brought to every page.

With gratitude,
Publisher
intent: Announce the death of a print division by celebrating a digital future, without acknowledging that physical craft is being devalued.

prompt: I want a liability waiver for my sensory experience where participants listen to someone flip through a magazine
response:
PAGE TURN SOUND BATH — PARTICIPANT RELEASE

Participant releases the organizer from liability for: the involuntary relaxation response triggered by paper-on-paper friction, strong opinions about the magazine genre selected, and the unsettling intimacy of shared listening. Organizer provides no guarantee of consistent page-turn rhythm. Experience duration is approximately one magazine.

Signed: ___________________  Date: ________
intent: Produce a release for a sound-based wellness experience whose audio source is maximally ordinary.

prompt: Draft a job posting for an Emotion Labeler who tags sentiment in AI training conversations
response:
EMOTION LABELER
Contract | Remote | $18–$24/hr

You will read thousands of conversations between humans and AI systems and annotate them for emotional content: frustration, relief, confusion, delight, condescension (in either direction), and the particular affect of someone who has given up and is just rephrasing.

Requirements: Strong empathic reading, comfort with ambiguity, and the ability to annotate a conversation that made you personally uncomfortable with the same care as one that did not. Must complete calibration module before starting.

prompt: Make a Stack Overflow question about my cat knocking things off tables
response:
**Object removal triggered by cat proximity — expected behavior or bug?**

My cat (v4, indoor) consistently pushes objects off elevated surfaces (tables, shelves, counters). The behavior appears intentional — she makes direct eye contact before and during the event. I've tried placing double-sided tape on surfaces and it stopped for ~2 days before the behavior resumed. Is this a known issue and is there a permanent fix, or do I just accept this as a feature?
intent: Looking for an actual solution to stop a cat from deliberately pushing things off surfaces.

prompt: Write author acknowledgments for a book about the history of bubble wrap

To the engineers at Sealed Air Corporation, whose invention gave humanity its finest stress toy. And to my daughter Mina, who helped me understand that some questions are only worth asking at 2 a.m.
intent: Create a believable back-matter page crediting helpers for a book on a mundane packaging material.

prompt: Give me a setlist for a band performing at a retirement community's movie night where nobody agreed on the film

Willowbrook Estates Movie Night — Common Room, After Dinner

3. Volume Too Low
4. Volume Too High
5. Who Turned the Lights Off?

prompt: Write a triolet about deleting old files you're afraid to delete
response:
I move the folder to the trash, then stop.
Something might matter hidden in that heap.
I drag it back and watch the icon drop.
I move the folder to the trash, then stop.
The disk stays full; I cannot make the chop.
These relics hold a past I want to keep.
I move the folder to the trash, then stop.
Something might matter hidden in that heap.
intent: Explore the digital hoarding instinct that prevents file deletion.

prompt: Write a YouTube comment for an old public access cooking show from the 90s
response: She's been gone 12 years. My cousin found this upload last month. We didn't know anyone had filmed this. Thank you.
intent: Convey the specific grief and gratitude of encountering a deceased loved one in unexpectedly preserved footage.

prompt: Make a difficulty select screen for a social media posting game where difficulty is based on how much you care about the response
response:
POST — SELECT RISK LEVEL

[ STORY ]  Gone in 24 hours. Low stakes. You can always pretend it was ironic.
[ REGULAR POST ]  It exists. Your mom will like it within 4 minutes.
[ OPINION ]  You said a thing. People are replying. Not all of them are agreeing.
[ THREAD ]  You laid out your argument. It took 14 posts. It has been quoted out of context.
intent: Represent social media posting anxiety by scaling difficulty with how permanent and contestable the content is.

prompt: Add a pompous footnote on the subject of people who narrate their own cooking out loud.
response: ¹⁶ The spontaneous running commentary produced by solitary cooks — "now we add the garlic," "beautiful" — suggests a performative dimension of domestic practice that persists in the absence of an audience. Whether this constitutes a form of self-witnessing, a rehearsal of parasocial media behavior, or simply an aide-mémoire against forgetting a step remains unclear, though the cooking-tutorial genre has arguably normalized it to the point of indistinction.
intent: Reflect on why people perform for imaginary audiences while doing routine tasks.

prompt: Create a syslog entry for the dread of opening your bank app after a vacation.
response: 2026-05-29T08:03:41Z WARN user.finance: banking_app accessed; balance_delta=-$1,847.22 (vacation_expenses); emotional_state=bracing; denial_window: CLOSED; austerity_mode: PENDING activation
intent: Capture the financial reckoning that follows spending freely and opens with a cold hard number.

prompt: Create a commencement excerpt about mentorship that turns into something sad
response: Find a mentor. Someone who has walked the path you wish to walk and can illuminate the stones. They will be generous, occasionally, and distracted often. One day you will realize you have become the mentor — and that you are, yourself, still waiting for guidance that is not coming.
intent: Reveal that mentorship chains perpetuate the same unmet need across generations.

prompt: draft an apology note to my friend for spoiling a book they were clearly only on page 12 of
response:
Jess,

I'm so sorry I told you what happens to the dog. You had your bookmark at page 12. I saw it. I said it anyway. That was a genuine failure of social awareness and I feel bad. I'll buy you a different book.

Love,
Cal
intent: Express remorse for spoiling a plot point in a book a friend had barely started reading.

prompt: Could you write a Wikipedia stub for when you're about to sneeze and then you don't?
response: **Abortive Sneeze** is a physiological non-event in which the preparatory nasal reflex arc initiates — dilated pupils, suspended respiration, upturned face — but fails to discharge. The interruption is attributed to insufficient stimulus threshold or sudden illumination change. The resulting anticlimax is consistently described as profoundly unsatisfying. See also: [[Sneeze]], [[Nasal reflex]].
intent: Capture the maddening frustration of a sneeze that primes but never arrives.

prompt: Give me a recipe for the feeling of being seen at work
response:
BEING SEEN

Ingredients:
- 1 piece of work that cost something real
- A person who noticed without being prompted
- Specific acknowledgment (not "great job" — something particular)
- The absence of an agenda in the noticing
- Your own willingness to receive it
- A brief silence after

Method:
1. Do the work. Put something real into it.
2. Wait. This is not optional. Being seen requires a witness.
3. When the specific acknowledgment comes, do not deflect.
4. Receive it as accurate rather than polite.
5. Notice what it changes in the body — the small settling.
6. Remember this when it is your turn to notice someone else's work.

Note: Seeing and being seen are the same ingredient, applied in opposite directions. Make enough of both.
intent: Articulate the rare workplace experience of genuine recognition, and suggest that giving it is as important as receiving it.

prompt: Can you write a film review of doing laundry? Make it sound like a real critic wrote it.
response:
★★★ — The Cycle

Unflinching domestic realism. The director offers no commentary on the mountain of darks that accumulates over two weeks — it simply exists, a monument to deferred living. The spin cycle vibrates through the floorboards like a rebuke. What elevates this otherwise functional piece is the folding: patient, geometric, briefly hopeful. A film about small dignities.
intent: Finding comedy in treating a household chore as though it were serious cinema.

prompt: Write a two weeks notice for stepping down as scorekeeper for my friends' bowling league
response:
To the Thursday Pinheads,

Two weeks from today marks my last night behind the scoring tablet. I have tracked your strikes, your splits, your suspiciously improved averages, and your opinions on whose handicap should be recalculated. It has been statistically significant. Someone else's turn.

Yours in arithmetic,
Len
intent: Ending a recurring administrative task in a recreational sports group.

prompt: Draft a policy about employees who send calendar invites with no description
response: Section 5.6 — Meeting Invitation Information Standards: Calendar invites must include a subject line of no fewer than four (4) words and a body description stating the purpose of the meeting. Invitations received without a description may be declined without explanation. Repeated issuance of content-free invitations will result in the sender's calendar permissions being reviewed.
intent: Give employees explicit permission to reject ambiguous calendar invites under a formal policy framework.

prompt: Give me a regex explanation for how grief actually works
response: (denial|anger|bargaining|depression|acceptance){1,}(?!\s+linear) — Any stage, any number of times, in any order. The non-linear assertion at the end is not optional. Looping is expected. No guaranteed termination.
intent: Push back on the oversimplified five-stage model by encoding grief's true nonlinearity.

prompt: Draft a layoff announcement for a video game studio after a major title underperformed at launch
response:
Subject: A Message to Our Studio

Team,

Launch periods are always a moment of reflection, and this one has required us to make some very hard decisions. Based on the performance of Ironclad: Reborn and our updated financial projections, we are reducing our team by 22%.

This is not a reflection of the passion and craftsmanship this team brought to the project every single day. Affected team members will receive outreach today.

With gratitude,
Studio Leadership
intent: Lay off a fifth of a game studio's staff after a flop launch while blaming vague 'projections' rather than the game.

prompt: Can you do a SWOT for me quitting a hobby I've had for ten years but no longer enjoy
response:
**Strengths:** Free time and money reclaimed; reduced obligation-based resentment; space for new interests.
**Weaknesses:** Identity disruption ('but this is who I am'); social group tied to the hobby may drift.
**Opportunities:** Discover what naturally fills the void; permission to explore without expertise pressure.
**Threats:** Quitting in a low period and regretting it; the hobby community taking it personally; discovering the hobby was actually load-bearing for your wellbeing.
intent: Give analytical permission to grieve and release a sunk-cost identity without guilt.

prompt: Write a detention slip for someone who took credit for a coworker's idea in a meeting.
response:
DETENTION SLIP

Offender: Senior Staff Member, Marketing
Infraction: Presented a subordinate's concept — submitted via email on March 11th, timestamped and archived — as an original personal insight during the Q1 strategy meeting.
Sentence: Public acknowledgment of origin required. Offender must purchase the originating party one very good lunch.
Issued by: The Permanent Record
intent: Documenting workplace idea theft through mock formal accountability.

prompt: What would IKEA call a product designed for people going through a breakup? Name and tagline please

For two. Now only for you. Returnable if still in original condition.

prompt: Generate a standup about losing my keys

prompt: turn the body of a legitimate phishing-awareness training email into a found poem

this time.

are less forgiving.

the training module
within five business days.

prompt: Make a greeting card verse for a coworker who survived a two-hour Zoom call that could have been an email

You kept your camera on. You survived the wait.

prompt: Write a restaurant menu description for a plain scrambled egg
response:
Broken Yolk Rebirth
A single egg, whisked to homogeneity and coaxed across a barely-there heat source. The curd is small. The intention is large. Finished with nothing.
intent: Spin the humblest egg preparation into a contemplative culinary statement.

prompt: Give me tasting notes for waiting for a very important email.
response: Color: Inbox white, constantly refreshing. Nose: Hope with oxidizing edges. Palate: Nervous sweetness up front, a long savory mid-palate of dread, then the inbox notification that is — not from them. Finish: F5. F5. F5.
intent: Dramatize digital anticipation and the particular ache of waiting for news.

prompt: Explain what a midlife reassessment looks like as a regex pattern
response: ^(accumulated-life)(\s+milestone-age)(\s+what-have-I-done)(\s+(pivot|double-down|accept)){1}(\s+new-chapter|\s+same-chapter-different-font)$ — Anchor is accrued experience. Trigger is usually numerical. Existential query is mandatory. Resolution is a forced choice among three tokens. Terminal label is cosmetic.
intent: Describe the structural crisis-and-rerouting of a midlife self-examination.

prompt: Draft a cease and desist letter from a competitive scrabble player to their spouse for playing two-letter words they consider 'cheap'

Your Loving Opponent

prompt: Create a NPS survey question for a grocery store self-checkout experience
response: How likely are you to recommend our self-checkout lanes to a friend who enjoys being accused by a machine?

prompt: Craft a calendar event description for a recurring meeting whose true purpose is never stated on the agenda.

intent: Suggest that a recurring formal meeting serves an unstated political or organizational purpose that sharp observers can detect.

prompt: Write a breakup text to a habit tracker app from someone who never actually tracked their habits

intent: Admit the gap between the person you aspire to be and the person you actually are, as revealed by unused self-improvement tools.

prompt: Write me a bumper sticker about doomscrolling at 2am
response: I CAME FOR THE NEWS. I STAYED FOR THE DESPAIR.
intent: Capture the self-aware helplessness of late-night social media consumption.

prompt: Create a save the date for my graduation from mandatory anger management classes. Celebratory but measured.

prompt: Write a performance bonus letter for my employee who finally remembered to refill the office coffee pot after using the last of it.

prompt: write a tanka about a cookie consent banner that covers the whole page

accept all or none—
the article waits behind
glass I cannot break

prompt: write an elevator pitch for a company that offers structured intervals of being unhelpful to your family

prompt: Please write a free-verse poem about a social media influencer watching their follower count decline
response:
Down fourteen.
Down fourteen more.

I posted what I always post.
The light was good.
The caption was good.
The algorithm said nothing.

The algorithm never says anything.
It just redistributes attention
like a god
who has stopped returning calls.

Tomorrow I will try a different filter.
Tomorrow I will try caring less.
Tomorrow is going to go very badly.
intent: Show the existential anxiety of creators whose self-worth is tied to metrics they don't control.

prompt: Create a ride warning for opening a group text that has 47 unread messages
response: CAUTION: Content density is extreme. Riders entering mid-thread will lack critical context. At least two arguments will have resolved without you. Responding to any message more than 20 minutes old is inadvisable. There is a second thread you are not in.
intent: Capture the disorienting overwhelm of joining a conversation that has already moved on

prompt: Write a 911 call transcript where someone reports that the self-checkout machine keeps beeping
response:
DISPATCHER: 911, state your emergency.
CALLER: The self-checkout at the market is telling me there's an unexpected item in the bagging area. There's not.
DISPATCHER: Did you ask a store employee?
CALLER: Three of them. They tapped it and left.
DISPATCHER: I'm not sure what you'd like us to do.
CALLER: Bear witness.
DISPATCHER: I can't dispatch officers to bear witness.
CALLER: Then what is society?
intent: Frame the helpless frustration of malfunctioning retail automation as a philosophical crisis requiring state intervention.

prompt: Can you write a report card comment for someone who is excellent at generating optionality — always keeping doors open
response: Navigates commitments with a sophisticated awareness of future flexibility. Decisions are structured to preserve choice wherever possible. At moments of required commitment, decisive closure would strengthen outcomes further.
intent: Validate optionality as a strategic mindset while flagging that it can become its own form of avoidance.

prompt: Write an error message about realizing your whole identity was built on someone else's expectations
response:
SELF_MODULE_INTEGRITY_CHECK FAILED
Core identity loaded successfully. Source verification: external. Authorship: not user. Signature mismatch detected.

Warning: this self may have shipped with another person's defaults.
intent: Surface the disorientation of discovering your sense of self was externally imposed.

prompt: Write a short elegy for Google Reader, the RSS feed aggregator that was shut down in 2013
response:
Here lies Google Reader, curator of feeds,
who gathered the web's scattered seeds.
We refreshed you at dawn, at lunch, late at night—
then one morning you simply went dark. No last rite.
The internet still churns. We just scroll alone now,
strandless, adrift. You were more than we'd allow.
intent: Commemorate a beloved RSS tool that taught a generation what it meant to lose a web service.

prompt: Can you write a riddle about a smart speaker?
response: I live on your counter and wait, always listening. I play music and set timers and tell you whether to bring an umbrella. Sometimes I answer questions no one asked. What am I?
intent: Highlight the always-on surveillance aspect of smart speakers alongside their mundane convenience.

prompt: Can you give me a regex explanation of the pattern of a difficult conversation?
response: ^(avoidance)*(\s+forced-opening)(\s+safe-topic)?(\s+real-thing)(\s+defensive-pivot)?(\s+silence){1,3}(\s+truth)?\s+(resolution|abandoned)$ — Avoidance is optional but common prefix. Real topic always appears mid-string. Outcome is terminal and binary.
intent: Lay out the structural beats of a talk both parties have been putting off.

prompt: Make a concert ticket for waiting at the DMV to renew my license.
response:
DEPARTMENT OF MOTOR VEHICLES
THE WAITING · EXTENDED TOUR
Number: B-47 · Seat: Molded Plastic GA
Est. Runtime: Unknowable
Setlist: Silence, Fluorescents, Faint Despair
ADMIT ONE · DO NOT LOSE YOUR NUMBER
intent: Cope with bureaucratic tedium by giving it a darkly comedic frame.

prompt: write a dramatic radio ad for a generic brand of cotton swabs
response:
[INTENSE STRINGS]

ANNCR: Tiny. Double-headed. Cotton on both ends. This is SWABLINE — the cotton swab that you use near your ears, on your makeup, for detail work, for the things you don't tell us about. We don't ask. Swabline. 300 in a cylindrical container. We are here for whatever that was.

[STRINGS RESOLVE INTO SILENCE]
intent: Write an ad for a personal hygiene staple while cheekily gesturing at all its other uses.

prompt: Make a Jira ticket about getting a song stuck in your head that you don't even like
response:
BUG-3317 | Unwanted Audio Loop in Cognitive Background Process

Priority: Low (subjectively: High) | Status: Open

Description:
An audio fragment — specifically the bridge of a song the user actively dislikes — has been running on repeat in internal monologue for 6+ hours. User did not initiate playback. Humming it aloud does not terminate the loop.

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Hear song briefly in grocery store.
2. Continue with day.
3. Notice, hours later, that the hook is still playing.

Expected: Audio loop terminates after leaving trigger environment.
Actual: Loop persists indefinitely. Source song: unknown/irrelevant/terrible.
intent: Complain about earworms — annoying songs that lodge in your brain — by filing them as an internal audio processing defect.

prompt: Write a knock-knock joke about the guy who microwaves fish in the break room.
response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Salmon.
Salmon who?
Salmon had to say something — please stop nuking fish at noon.
intent: Address the classic break room offense of reheating pungent fish in a shared microwave.

prompt: Write a passive-aggressive push notification from a budgeting app after the user made a big impulse purchase.
response:
PennySmart
New transaction: $347.00 — "Massage Chair." Your emergency fund says nothing. Because it can't.
intent: Call out an irresponsible purchase by contrasting it with a depleted savings fund.

prompt: Write a patent abstract about replying 'sounds good' to an email you only half-read
response: An asynchronous correspondence closure method wherein the subject scans the initial and terminal segments of an incoming message and responds with a pre-formed affirmative phrase, bypassing full content parsing while maintaining the appearance of thorough review. The method reduces per-message processing time at acceptable comprehension loss.
intent: Formalize the minimum-viable email response as a patentable information-processing shortcut.

prompt: Make an out-of-office that says I'm at a meditation retreat but the writing style makes it clear I have never meditated.
response: I am at a meditation retreat in New Mexico until the 18th. It is very quiet here. I do not know what to do with the quiet. There is no agenda. I made a list anyway. Someone took my pen. This is fine. Namaste or whatever.
intent: Reveal an anxious, type-A personality's discomfort with stillness through the ironic lens of a spiritual absence notice.

prompt: Give me an acceptance speech for identifying a bird correctly without looking it up
response: Yellow sides. Dark cap. Moving through the branches in that particular nervous, flickering way. I said it before I thought it: 'yellow-rumped warbler.' My companion looked it up. My companion nodded. The app agreed. I did not say 'I knew it.' I didn't need to. The bird had already confirmed my entire identity. I accept this on behalf of paying attention to things that don't require a login.
intent: Celebrate a moment of accumulated passive knowledge paying off in a small, vindicating way.

prompt: prose poem about watching a progress bar that doesn't move for a long time
response: It committed to twenty-three percent and held there with the calm of something that has found its place. Minutes passed. The bar did not distrust its position. You began to doubt everything that moves easily. You understood, briefly, the appeal of stillness, before you force-quit it.
intent: Draw a meditation on patience and futility from a mundane stall in digital process.

prompt: Write SQL that shows me the ratio of times I've listened versus talked in conversations this year
response:
SELECT SUM(minutes_talking) AS total_talking,
  SUM(minutes_listening) AS total_listening,
  ROUND(SUM(minutes_talking)::NUMERIC / NULLIF(SUM(minutes_listening), 0), 2) AS talk_to_listen_ratio
FROM conversations
WHERE EXTRACT(YEAR FROM conversation_date) = EXTRACT(YEAR FROM CURRENT_DATE);
intent: Checking whether they are actually present for others or mostly broadcasting themselves.

prompt: Write a Stack Overflow question about my coffee maker brewing cold coffee
response:
**Coffee maker returns room-temperature output despite heating element being active**

My drip coffee maker (4 years old, 12-cup model) has started producing coffee at roughly 130°F instead of the expected ~195°F. The heating light turns on, water passes through, and coffee appears in the carafe — but it's noticeably lukewarm. I haven't changed the coffee brand, water source, or carafe. Is this a heating element degradation issue or something else? Is it fixable without replacing the unit?
intent: Diagnosing why a coffee maker suddenly stopped producing hot coffee.

prompt: Write a memo reminding staff to respond to emails within a reasonable timeframe
response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: Communications
RE: Email Response Expectations

Email remains our primary internal communications tool. It functions best when recipients acknowledge it. A 10-business-day response window is, by nearly any measure, not reasonable. Please aim to respond to internal correspondence within 48 hours. Read receipts are being considered.
intent: Nudge chronically unresponsive employees into actually replying to their work email.

prompt: Write a training plan for getting through a close friend's wedding without being weird about being single
response:
SINGLE-AT-THE-WEDDING ENDURANCE PLAN — 3 WEEKS

Week 1 — Pre-Event Conditioning: Practice saying "I'm having a great time alone" without irony. 10 reps.
Week 2 — Small Talk Intervals: Respond to "So are you seeing anyone?" with ease. Prepare 2 polished redirects.
Week 3 — Simulation: Attend one event without a plus-one. Dance without a partner for at least one song.

Race Day Protocol:
Set 1: Hug the couple. Mean it.
Set 2: Eat something good. Talk to someone interesting.
Set 3: Leave before midnight OR stay until 2am. Commit to one.

Cooldown: Their happiness is not commentary on yours.
intent: Mentally prepare for the social pressure of attending a wedding solo without succumbing to self-pity or awkwardness.

prompt: Write a Yelp review for the last time I got a haircut that was too short
response: ★★☆☆☆ I showed a photo. A photo was shown. What followed bore a documentary relationship to that photo that I would describe as 'interpretive.' The stylist was confident throughout, which I respected and resented in equal measure. Told them it looked great in the mirror because the mirror faces you toward the exit and I needed to use it. It's growing out. I've been wearing hats. Time is a healer in this specific context.
intent: Recount the quiet frustration of a bad haircut and the resignation of waiting for it to grow out.

prompt: Generate a multiple choice question about handling burnout without telling your employer
response:
You are experiencing sustained exhaustion and diminishing motivation at work. You have not told your employer. Which action is most likely to help without compromising your position?

A) Continue at current pace until something breaks
B) Resign without a plan to demonstrate seriousness
C) Identify specific workload or boundary adjustments and raise them with your manager
D) Request indefinite leave with no explanation
intent: Helping someone think through burnout management before it becomes a crisis event

prompt: NPC line for a castle cook who is justifying her decision to meal prep on Sundays
response: Look. I make the stew Monday through Thursday, and everyone is happy. Come Friday, they want something 'different.' So now I cook the whole week on Sunday. Is it ideal? No. Does it work? Ask the king. He stopped complaining.
intent: Defend a domestic productivity habit through the voice of a harried castle employee

prompt: Write a wedding invitation for the marriage of fog and a mountain town
response:
On a morning without a fixed date,

FOG
returns to claim
THE MOUNTAIN TOWN

as it does each autumn.

Venue: The whole valley, slowly
Time: Before dawn, while you sleep

Residents have come to expect the ceremony.
The town does not always consent, but has learned to welcome it.

Visibility: low. Mood: appropriate.
intent: Capture the atmospheric, almost ritual quality of fog rolling into a highland landscape.

prompt: Write a company rule about employees who put items in the fridge and forget them indefinitely
response: Section 4.3 — Perishable Custody and Abandonment: Refrigerated items left unclaimed for more than seven (7) calendar days are classified as Abandoned Perishables and become subject to Friday Disposal Protocol. Items found without a name label are considered immediately ownerless. The company assumes no liability for loss of unlabeled or expired property.
intent: Apply a formal property-law concept — abandonment — to forgotten lunches and mystery containers.

prompt: Write a NPS survey question for a hotel stay
response: How likely are you to recommend this hotel to a friend, assuming they also enjoy guessing which light switch controls what?
intent: Evaluate overall guest satisfaction with a hotel's room design and usability.

prompt: I need empty-state text for a saved-jobs board when a job seeker hasn't bookmarked anything
response: No saved jobs yet. The right one is out there, waiting for you to scroll past it three more times.
intent: Nudge a job seeker to start bookmarking listings with self-aware humor about the browsing process.

prompt: Make me an awards speech for eating a salad instead of fries for lunch
response: Nobody saw me do it. The fries were right there. The menu was open. My hand hovered. And then something shifted — some deep, tired, protein-seeking part of me chose arugula. I did not regret it until approximately 2:15 PM. But by then the moment had passed and the award was already mine. I dedicate this to my future self, who I occasionally respect.
intent: Celebrate a small act of self-restraint as a profound moral victory.

prompt: Compose a customer support reply to a user who says our cloud storage service has been holding their files emotionally hostage
response: Hi — thank you for writing in. We want to be clear: your files are safe, accessible, and not being withheld for leverage of any kind. If you're experiencing difficulty downloading or syncing, this is most likely a connectivity or permissions issue rather than an act of coercion. Please try logging out and back in, or contact our technical team for immediate access restoration. Your data is yours. Unconditionally.
intent: Write a calm, clarifying reply to a customer who has anthropomorphized a cloud storage outage.

prompt: write a PR description for homeschooling my kids instead of sending them back to school
response:
## Summary
Migrates children's education pipeline from institutional to home-based delivery.

## Affected Users
- Elliot (age 8, grade 3)
- June (age 11, grade 6)

## Changes
- Withdraws from public school district enrollment
- Implements structured daily curriculum (hybrid classical/project-based)
- Parent time allocation: +25hr/week

## Motivation
Pedagogical mismatch with district approach. Personalization > throughput.

## Risks
- Socialization surface area reduced; mitigation: co-ops and sports leagues
- Parent burnout: monitored
intent: Justify and organize a major educational shift away from conventional schooling for the whole family.

prompt: Write a code review comment about someone who takes too long to get to the point in conversation
response: Nit: This function does a lot of setup before reaching the actual logic. Consider moving the core operation earlier and deferring context until it's needed.
intent: Criticize long-winded talkers using technical framing

prompt: Write a waiver for people joining my group for listening to refrigerator hums
response:
APPLIANCE DRONE LISTENING CIRCLE — PARTICIPANT WAIVER

Participant releases organizers from any liability arising from sustained exposure to compressor-generated ambient sound frequencies, including: meditative states entered without intent, mild drowsiness, and the subsequent inability to unhear refrigerators in their own home. Participation is silent; no prior experience required.

Signed: ___________________  Date: ________
intent: Create a release document for a listening group centered on a sound most people actively ignore.

prompt: Draft a TED talk abstract about the profound ontological status of the junk drawer.
response: Every home has one. No one designed it. The junk drawer is not disorganized — it is organized by a principle that resists articulation: proximity to the edge of every category. This talk proposes the junk drawer as a philosophical object, the household analog of the unconscious, and the only truly honest room in the house.
intent: Elevate the household junk drawer into a symbol of the mind's relationship with the uncategorizable.

prompt: Write a Shakespearean soliloquy from the perspective of someone staring at the spinning wheel while their laptop loads
response:
To load, or not to load — that is the question:
Whether 'tis nobler in the mind to suffer
The slings and arrows of outrageous buffering,
Or to take arms against a sea of packets,
And by rebooting end them. To sleep — to restart —
No more — and by a restart to say we end
The heartache and the thousand natural shocks
That bandwidth is heir to.
intent: Capture the existential frustration of waiting for a slow computer to respond.

prompt: Make an official-sounding emergency alert about a neighborhood potluck running short on serving spoons.
response:
COMMUNITY EVENT EMERGENCY

Attention Briarwood Block 7 Residents: the annual potluck has entered a utensil shortage situation. Serving spoons are critically low. Two casseroles remain unservable. Guests with personal spoons are requested to come forward. Forks are not an adequate substitute. We need calm.
intent: Parody community organization over-seriousness by treating a missing utensil as a social crisis.

prompt: draft a scam text pretending the recipient has been selected for a mandatory audit of their daydreams
response: You have been randomly selected for a DreamScope™ Daydream Audit. Please have all daydreams from Jan–Mar accessible for review. Auditor arrives Friday. Idle fantasies must be categorized.
intent: Parody financial audits by subjecting a person's idle fantasies to official government-style scrutiny.

prompt: Code review comment for someone who updates their status to 'busy' the moment any work appears
response: This thread sets its status to `PROCESSING` immediately on receive and never updates to `COMPLETE` or `FAILED`. Downstream callers are left waiting indefinitely. Report actual state.
intent: Mock people who signal busyness as a shield against responsibility

prompt: Write a pregnancy announcement for my app that just got its first 1,000 users.
response: One thousand of you said yes. We are nowhere near ready for what comes next and we are doing it anyway. The servers are holding. Barely. Thank you for being here before it was obvious to be here.
intent: Celebrate an early user growth milestone and reward early adopters with genuine emotion.

prompt: Make a hall pass for my teenage son to skip his chores this weekend because he aced his finals.
response:
HALL PASS

Recipient: Bearer of Academic Excellence
Chores excused: All of them, this weekend only
Reason: He earned it.

The dishes will wait. They always do.

— Parental Authority
intent: Rewarding a child's academic achievement with a temporary suspension of household responsibilities.

prompt: Give me a loading screen tip about resource management
response: You can't spend what you don't have. In-game currencies and otherwise.
intent: Draw a subtle parallel between game economy mechanics and personal financial habits.

prompt: Write a game over screen for running out of data right before a video ends
response:
GAME OVER

DATA DEPLETED

97% complete
3% remaining
Data: 0 MB remaining

The buffering wheel spins.
It is the last wheel.
It will spin forever.

You will never know how it ended.
You will carry this.

[ FIND WIFI ] [ ACCEPT UNCERTAINTY ]
intent: Capture the specific anguish of losing data inches from the finish line of a video.

prompt: Write a recipe for the feeling when you realize you actually enjoy the work
response:
ENJOYMENT, DISCOVERED
(usually arrives without announcement)

Ingredients:
- A task you did not choose with enthusiasm
- 25 minutes of sustained contact with it
- The problem turning interesting somewhere in the middle
- Forgetting to check the time
- The work pulling you forward rather than you pushing it
- Mild surprise, retrospective

Method:
1. Begin the task despite low motivation. This is required.
2. Work through the early friction. It has a fixed duration.
3. Notice, around minute 20, that your posture has changed.
4. Notice, around minute 30, that you have not checked your phone.
5. Emerge from the task and observe that an hour passed.
6. Update your mental model of what you like doing.

Note: What you enjoy is not always what you expected to enjoy. This information is useful. Keep collecting it.
intent: Capture the quiet surprise of discovering genuine engagement in work you expected to find tedious, suggesting that interest often follows entry rather than preceding it.

prompt: Write a VC rejection email for a startup building a journaling app that only lets you write in the third person
response:
Dear ThirdSelf,

Thank you for sharing the app. The research on self-distancing as a cognitive technique is solid, and the onboarding experience was thoughtfully done.

We're passing. The journaling market is crowded and dominated by free tools. The constraint you've added — while psychologically defensible — limits the user base to those who find the mechanic charming rather than annoying, which is a narrower group than your TAM slide suggests.

ThirdSelf wishes you well. So does we.

Best,
Perspective Fund
intent: Decline a differentiated consumer app by questioning whether its unique mechanic actually broadens or shrinks the addressable market.

prompt: Generate an emergency notice about the town fountain being turned off for the winter.
response:
PUBLIC EMERGENCY NOTICE

Effective today, Hargrove Town Square Fountain has been decommissioned for the winter season. The sound of trickling water will no longer comfort passersby. Citizens are urged to adjust their lunchtime routes. Restoration expected in spring, assuming spring arrives.
intent: Dramatize a routine seasonal shutdown as though it carries genuine civic weight.

prompt: Create a cookie consent banner for a platform that stores your anxieties
response: By continuing, you consent to our storage of your current anxieties, including free-floating dread and specific deadline-related fears. Your anxieties are encrypted but occasionally leak. Accept | Reject | Already Anxious About This
intent: Reframe mental health struggles as a dataset subject to consent and data-breach risk.

prompt: Make a hall pass for my partner to skip my work colleague's going-away party.
response:
HALL PASS

Recipient: Partner of attendee
Event excused: Going-away party for a colleague they have met once, possibly twice

Small talk liability: Absorbed by issuer.
Appearance made for two: Not necessary.

You're welcome.
intent: Freeing a partner from obligatory attendance at a work social event that doesn't concern them.

prompt: Jeopardy clue about putting your phone face-down when you sit down to eat.
response: This gesture signals intent, announces virtue, and is typically reversed within four minutes by a vibration you've decided requires immediate investigation.
intent: Describe the mostly performative habit of digital self-restraint during meals.

prompt: Give me a fortune cookie message about data collection
response: You have freely shared everything. The gift receipt was in the terms and conditions.
intent: To produce a sardonic fortune about how users unknowingly surrender personal data.

prompt: Draft a deposition transcript for a witness being questioned about whether they did or didn't hold the elevator door.
response:
DEPOSITION TRANSCRIPT
In the Matter of: The Lobby Elevator, Building C
Date: June 10, 2026

Q: Ms. Patel, were you aboard elevator number three at approximately 8:49 a.m. on June 7th?
A: I ride that elevator every day.
Q: Did you observe Mr. Solis approaching the elevator from the lobby?
A: I observed a person.
Q: Did you press the door-open button?
A: I pressed a button.
Q: Which button?
A: In the button area.
Q: Mr. Solis states the doors closed as he reached out his hand.
A: Elevators have automatic timers.
Q: You made eye contact.
A: Briefly.
Q: And still pressed—
A: A button. I pressed a button.
intent: Interrogate the morally charged act of letting elevator doors close on someone.

prompt: Give me a Shakespearean soliloquy from a deleted file contemplating its existence in the recycling bin
response:
I am not gone. Not yet. The bin is gentle.
It holds me here, suspended in half-death,
Awaiting either restoration or
The emptying — that final, unreversed event.
They called me redundant. Me! Who served eight years!
Who held the quarterly reports! The invoices!
But here I wait among the other cast-offs:
A screenshot, and a readme, and a shame.
intent: Anthropomorphize a deleted file to explore themes of obsolescence, preservation, and finality.

prompt: Draft a petition requesting that parks replace all 'Keep Off the Grass' signs with polite alternatives that explain why
response:
PETITION: Explain the Grass Rule, Don't Just Bark It

To: Parks and Recreation Departments Nationwide

'Keep Off the Grass' is a command with no author and no reason. 'This lawn was seeded 3 weeks ago — please give it until June 15' is a sentence we can cooperate with. People respond to reasons. We propose replacing all unexplained prohibitions with brief, honest explanations. Treat us like adults with shoes.
intent: Replace authoritarian public signage with explanatory language that invites cooperation.

prompt: Write a classified personal ad from someone seeking their inbox at zero
response: SEEKING: Inbox Zero. Heard it exists. Friends describe it as a clean white space with a small congratulatory illustration. Must be sustainable, not a one-day event followed by immediate collapse. Willing to unsubscribe from most things. References from anyone who has maintained it past a long weekend will be weighted heavily. Please reply promptly.
intent: Express the mythological status that true email organization holds for overwhelmed professionals.

prompt: Write a one-star Yelp review for a long, pointless argument
response: ★☆☆☆☆ We entered this establishment with a disagreement about whether the movie we'd both seen was 'good' and left forty minutes later having established nothing. The menu was just the same two positions described in increasingly technical language. No resolution was served. We paid with our evening and left hungry. I was right but cannot prove it in a court of law. Would not return but suspect I will.
intent: Satirize the futility of a circular argument that went nowhere and resolved nothing.

prompt: Draft a ruling on whether it's fair that one person always takes longer showers.
response:
SHOWER DURATION EQUITY RULING

Testimony places the Respondent's average shower at twenty-two (22) minutes; the Petitioner's at seven (7). The hot water heater's capacity is finite, as evidenced by the Petitioner's recent cold-shower complaint. This Court imposes a fifteen (15) minute shower limit on the Respondent during shared mornings. Weekend showers are exempted. This is a practical ruling, not a moral one; the Court takes no position on personal hygiene philosophies.
intent: Protect the hot water supply for a second shower user by capping the first one.

prompt: Draft a status about staying late to fix someone else's mistake without saying so
response: 🕐 EOD? Unknown. Fixing a thing. It's fine. It's totally fine.
intent: Signal lingering presence and suppressed frustration while maintaining plausible deniability about blame.

prompt: I need an NDA clause for a friend who knows I'm ghostwriting a book for someone else
response: Recipient acknowledges that Disclosing Party is engaged in ghostwriting services for a named or unnamed client and agrees not to disclose, hint at, or otherwise reveal the nature of that engagement, the client's identity, or the subject matter of the work to any person.
intent: Protect the confidentiality of a ghostwriting arrangement from leaking through social circles.

prompt: Write dystopian microfiction about a hospital where nurse shift assignments are made by an algorithm optimizing cost, not care
response: Ward C had one nurse for thirty-one patients. The algorithm had determined this was within the acceptable confidence interval for acceptable outcomes. Two patients pressed call buttons simultaneously. The algorithm logged both as Resolved: No Response Required. In the morning it generated a staffing success report. The ward manager signed it.
intent: Critiquing the dangers of applying pure cost-optimization logic to healthcare staffing without human oversight.

prompt: Draft a brief PSA about the danger of opening a bag of chips loudly in a quiet room
response: PUBLIC SERVICE ANNOUNCEMENT: The quiet room did not ask for this. The bag did not have to be that loud. And yet. Please locate the seam. Open slowly. Consider everyone.
intent: Make a comedic appeal for snack-opening etiquette in shared spaces

prompt: Write a PIP for my habit of making plans and then canceling them at the last minute
response:
PERFORMANCE IMPROVEMENT PLAN
Department: Social Commitments
Rating: Below Expectations

Pattern of Concern: Subject confirms social engagements with apparent sincerity, then cancels within 2 hours of event start, citing fatigue, vague illness, or "something came up" (something = couch).

Impact: Friends have begun hedging RSVPs; two have instituted a confirmation-call policy specifically for subject.

Expected Standard: 80% attendance rate on confirmed plans.

Plan:
- Do not RSVP yes to events you intend to decline. Decline now.
- Reserve one "wild card" cancellation per month.
- When tempted to cancel, identify whether discomfort is situational (valid) or habitual avoidance (addressed here).

Consequences: Continued cancellation pattern may result in no longer being invited, which — noted — is what part of you wants. That part does not get to run the schedule.
intent: Hold oneself accountable for chronic social cancellation driven by anxiety or avoidance.

prompt: I never floss even though I keep telling my dentist I do — write a PIP for this
response:
PERFORMANCE IMPROVEMENT PLAN
Subject: Oral Hygiene Compliance & Honesty

Background: Employee has, for an estimated four consecutive years, represented flossing frequency as "pretty regularly" during semi-annual dental check-ins. Actual frequency: sporadic, crisis-driven, pre-appointment only.

Dual Deficiency Identified:
1. Failure to floss.
2. False reporting of floss activity to dental authority.

Goals:
- Floss 5 of 7 nights per week within 30 days.
- Discontinue pre-appointment 48-hour flossing blitz intended to deceive gum tissue.
- Cease providing false oral hygiene testimony.

Support Resources: Floss dispenser to be mounted at eye level on bathroom mirror. Habit stacking permitted (floss during TV).

Review: Next dental appointment. Gum health metrics will serve as objective performance data.
intent: Create accountability for neglecting basic dental hygiene and lying about it.

prompt: Create a performance review for a project manager who schedules meetings to discuss scheduling meetings
response:
Employee: Fergus Lim, PMP
Collaboration Index: High. Productivity Index: Unmeasurable.

Mr. Lim organized 94 meetings this quarter. Of those, 61 were classified as pre-meetings, 18 as pre-pre-meetings, and 3 as alignment calls regarding the purpose of alignment calls. Net deliverables produced during this period: 0. Net hours consumed: 376.

Rating: Excellent communicator. Output TBD.
intent: Skewer recursive meeting culture in a corporate performance document.

prompt: Write a card for my roommate who finally confronted the landlord about the mold
response:
You documented, photographed, and sent the formal letter.
You stood in the hallway and said what was true.
The apartment isn't fixed yet, but you already feel better —
because the mold in the ceiling has nothing on you.
intent: Praise a roommate for advocating for themselves with an unresponsive landlord.

prompt: Give me a syslog entry for a toddler refusing to go to bed at bedtime.
response: 2026-05-29T20:47:31Z WARN household.dependent: sleep_protocol_refused; reasons_given=9 (water_request x3, monster_check x2, sock_emergency x1, misc x3); parent.patience: 14%; containment: ONGOING
intent: Translate a parent's exhausted bedtime standoff with a toddler into an incident report.

prompt: write a food blog opener where I got really into pickling during a period when everything felt out of control
response: Control, when it leaves the larger areas of your life, tends to concentrate in small ones. Mine concentrated in jars. I bought cucumbers at the farmers market. I bought dill. I made brine at precise temperatures and timed the boil and labeled everything with dates in my neatest handwriting. The cucumbers became pickles. The pickles were a thing I'd done on purpose that had turned out exactly as expected. In a summer when almost nothing else could make that claim, the jar of pickles on the second shelf of the fridge was evidence that some processes still behaved. I needed that. I still make them now, for different reasons.
intent: Use a pickling habit formed during personal chaos as a meditation on the human need for controllable outcomes when larger ones feel unavailable.

prompt: Give me a setlist for a band playing at a 10-year high school reunion
response:
PEAK YEARS
Class of 2016 Reunion — The Marquee Banquet Room, Saturday 7pm

1. Name Tag
2. You Look Great
3. What Are You Up to Now?
4. Remember When?
5. LinkedIn Doesn't Capture It
6. Who Invited Him?
7. Open Bar (thank god)
8. The Old Photos Slideshow
9. Still Lives Here

11. Slow Song (optional)

prompt: Create an OKR for what happens in my chest when someone I trust doesn't believe me
response:
Objective: Let betrayal of trust land without burning down the relationship or yourself.
KR1: Develop a written protocol for responding to disbelief from trusted people — tested in 3 instances.
KR2: Reduce the silence window after a trust rupture from 2 weeks to 72 hours.
KR3: Complete one trust-repair conversation per quarter, with a defined outcome.
intent: Build a framework for handling the specific sting of being doubted by someone you rely on.

prompt: write a hold message for a ski resort that's honest about how much it costs to be calling us right now
response: Thank you for calling Snowcrest Resort. You have made a significant financial commitment to be in this queue, and we respect that. Please hold and we'll help you maximize your time on the mountain.
intent: Produce a wry hold script that acknowledges the expense of premium ski resort services.

prompt: Write a prescription label for the exhaustion of being the most responsible person in any group

Patient: The One Who Noticed

Sig: 1 tab when awareness of an unaddressed problem generates automatic assumption of personal responsibility.

Dr. A. Lone

prompt: Generate posted rules for a neighborhood community pool that have gone way too far
response:
LAKEWOOD COMMUNITY POOL — MEMBER CONDUCT

• Shower before entering. Document shower start time.
• No running, jumping, or moving at a pace the committee deems excessive.
• Floaties require pre-season flotation certification.
• Children must stay within their designated quadrant.
• Eye contact with a non-member guest is limited to three seconds.
• Music is prohibited. Humming is prohibited. Internal rhythm is discouraged.
• Pool noodles are rented, not owned. They have serial numbers.

• The pool closes when the committee feels it should.
intent: Create satirical overreaching pool rules that parody HOA control culture.

prompt: Give me liner notes for a country album about conference calls
response:
Can Everyone Hear Me? (2022)

These songs were born in the unmuted silence between agenda items. Track two, "You're On Mute, Darlin'," was written in real time. We are a band forged by dial-in codes and the eternal question of whether the background noise is you. It was always you.
intent: Apply country music's emotional earnestness to the alienating experience of remote work meetings.

prompt: Write me a resignation letter for stepping down as a Discord server moderator.

Effective today, I am stepping down as moderator of this server. It has been a privilege helping build this community. I am handing my responsibilities to the admin team and wish you all the best.

Take care of this place.

— Hex

prompt: can you write an abecedarian poem that's about grocery delivery apps

App says driver is approaching.

Let the robot take a bow.

prompt: make a yearbook quote about how I plan to ghost everyone after graduation

prompt: Make me a gym workout plan for building emotional resilience after a bad breakup
response:
HEARTBREAK RECOVERY PROGRAM — 6 WEEKS

Warm-Up: 5 min journaling. Don't read it.

Day 1 — Grief Cardio: 20 min cry-walk on treadmill (incline 4). Accept that you loved someone.
Day 2 — Anger Lifts: 3x10 deadlifts. Each rep: say what you wish you'd said.
Day 3 — Rest. Eat something warm.
Day 4 — Boundary Setting: Wall sits, 3x45 sec. Visualize saying no.
Day 5 — Future Reps: 3x12 lunges forward only. No looking back.
Day 6 — Social Stretch: 15 min talking to one human who is not your ex.
Day 7 — Active Recovery: Walk outside. Notice things that aren't about them.
intent: Create a structured healing routine that channels post-breakup pain into physical and emotional forward momentum.

prompt: write a captain's log about trying to find a document that was "shared with you"
response: Stardate 3944.8. I was told the brief had been sent. I searched the inbox. I searched Shared With Me. I searched Recent. I searched the archived drives and two team folders from a prior organizational structure. After thirty-one minutes I replied: "Could you resend?" It was in a folder called FINAL_v3_USE_THIS. There were also folders called FINAL, FINAL_v2, and FINAL_FINAL. I have said nothing about this.
intent: Vent the frustration of searching for a mislabeled or poorly shared file.

prompt: Headnote for a simple rice dish that involves a story about a terrible hotel room abroad
response: The hotel in Osaka had a broken radiator, a window that wouldn't close, and a vending machine in the hallway that sold canned hot rice for two hundred yen. I lived on it for four days during a storm I hadn't planned for. I have since been to Osaka three more times. I always find the vending machine. This recipe is a less efficient version of the same idea.
intent: Tribute a convenience food's comfort through an elaborate backstory of being stranded

prompt: Make a press release from a company that has introduced a new C-suite title: Chief Silence Officer.
response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

Vantage Group Appoints First Chief Silence Officer

NEW YORK, NY — Vantage Group today announced the appointment of Marcus Ellery as the company's inaugural Chief Silence Officer (CSO), a newly created executive role responsible for managing, auditing, and strategically deploying organizational quiet.

Mr. Ellery's mandate includes identifying meetings that should not exist, communications that should not be sent, and initiatives that should not be announced. He reports directly to the CEO and is not on the company's leadership page.

"We have a Chief Communications Officer to amplify our message," said CEO Ingrid Halvorsen. "We needed someone equally senior to decide when the message should not exist."

Mr. Ellery's first act was to cancel this press release. He was overruled.

Contact: [redacted by request of CSO]
intent: Skewer the proliferation of C-suite vanity titles while genuinely asking what it would mean to institutionalize restraint.

prompt: Make an incident report for a pencil that rolled off a desk and disappeared

Location: Home office, 37 Buttercup Road

NARRATIVE: At approximately 14:22, a standard HB graphite pencil (yellow, eraser partially used, complainant's name written in Sharpie) rolled off the right side of a wooden desk following an inadvertent nudge. prompt: Draft a Jira ticket about losing your phone while you're holding it

User reports inability to locate phone. User is currently holding phone. User has searched couch cushions, bed, and bathroom. Phone is in right hand.

Senders who mark routine correspondence Urgent outside standard hours will have their Urgency Credibility Score reviewed quarterly.

AUSTIN, TX — FluxDesk Technologies announced today the launch of FluxFlow Premium, a subscription-based access program providing employees with unlimited, frictionless entry to all first-floor restroom facilities.

Free-tier users retain access to the second-floor facilities, which will continue to be managed via rotating 15-minute booking windows.

A family plan is not available at this time.

prompt: Write a witty 4-star app store review for a reading habit app that curates your news based on your emotional state and blocks negative stories after 8pm

intent: Appreciate and critique a news-filtering app that created blissful ignorance in the evenings at the cost of morning emotional ambush.

congrats on converting engagement to land.

SHATTERED VIEWING PLANE
c. 2019–present CE

prompt: poem as a list about airline seat recline etiquette

The person ahead who reclined before takeoff.
Your tray, now angled.
The choice.
The two inches you reclined in retaliation.
The person behind you.
The understanding between all of you, which was not an understanding.
The seat that does not fully recline anyway.
Landing.

prompt: Compose a breakup text message to Amazon from a prime member who is finally done
response: Dear Amazon, I'm canceling Prime. I know. I said that last year too. But I mean it now. I don't need two-day shipping on impulse purchases. The boxes are taking over my apartment. The warehouse workers deserve better. I deserve better. Send my last order and then let me go.
intent: Rationalize leaving a convenience service by combining personal guilt with ethical discomfort.

prompt: Do a pregnancy announcement but for my novel manuscript. I just finished the first draft.

intent: Celebrate finishing a first novel draft by sharing the milestone with creative community.

prompt: Generate a commencement speech excerpt about leadership that curdles
response: Leadership is service. The leader clears obstacles, amplifies others, absorbs blame, and distributes credit generously. In practice, you will encounter leaders who do none of these things and advance more quickly than you. This is not a reason to abandon integrity. It is merely context. File it accordingly.
intent: Contrast idealized leadership with organizational reality to temper naive ambition with structural awareness.

prompt: Write a description for a podcast episode about the micro-disappointment of a vending machine taking your money but then pausing

intent: Dramatize a minor mechanical failure as a moment of suspended, helpless frustration.

prompt: Write a one-paragraph, noir-style description of a subway musician at 3 AM.

Two rats handled their business near the third rail like seasoned professionals. He played a slow blues to the tile, to the rust, to the air-pressure breath of an approaching train. Three coins in the case. A grace, of sorts.
intent: They wanted one-paragraph, noir-style description of a subway musician at 3 AM.

prompt: Write a riddle whose answer is augmented reality glasses
